messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show resources command do?

The show resources command shows hardware resource consumption for the specified line modules or for all line modules. Resource data is updated every 10 seconds. Public ACL and Policy hardware resource commands 190 Hardware resource consumption information is shown for: • TCAM entries • Policers • L4 Port Ranges Specifies the member and slot of the line module. For example, to specify the line module in member 1, slot 2, enter 1/2. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show resources [<SLOT-ID>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip proxy-arp command do?

The ip proxy-arp command enables proxy ARP for the specified Layer 3 interface. Proxy ARP is supported on Layer 3 physical interfaces, LAG interfaces, and VLAN interfaces. It is disabled by default. To enable proxy ARP on an interface, routing must be enabled on that interface. The no form of this command disables proxy ARP for the specified interface. Syntax: no ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command enables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. BFD is asynchronous and echo mode is supported. The no form of this command disables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. Specifies the address on which to enable BFD in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no bfd <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd all-interfaces command do?

The bfd all-interfaces command enables BFD on all OSPFv2 or OSPFv3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables BFD on all active OSPFv2/OSPFv3 or IPv4/IPv6 interfaces, excluding those on which BFD was enabled at the interface level with the commands ip ospf bfd and IPv6 o spfv3 bfd. Syntax: no bfd all-interfaces.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd echo disable command do?

The bfd echo disable command disables support for BFD echo packets. Echo packet support is enabled by default. Public bfd echo disable 246 The no form of this command enables support for BFD echo packets. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Syntax: no bfd echo disable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR> command sets the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets. This address is used in all echo sessions. NOTE The source IP address must not be on the same network segment as any switch interface, otherwise a large number of ICMP redirect packets may be sent by the remote device, causing network congestion. The no form of this command removes the source IPv4 address for BFD echo packets, which causes the switch to stop sending echo packets. When a valid value is set, all sessions with a peer that is capable of receiving echo packets, will start transmitting echo packets. BFD control sessions continue to run concurrently with echo packets. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no bfd echo-src-ip-address <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD echo packets. The no form of this command removes the configured BFD echo packets interval. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. <INTERVAL> Specifies the minimum reception interval in milliseconds. A valu e of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD echo packets. Range: 0, 50 to 1000. Default: 500. Public bfd min-echo-receive-interval 249. Syntax: no bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD control packets on an interface. Public bfd min-receive-interval 250 The no form of this command removes the configured BFD minimum interval on an interface. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. Specifies the minimum receive interval in milliseconds. A value of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD c ontrol packets. Range: 500 to 20000. Default: 3000. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf <VRF-NAME> command creates a VRF instance named <VRF-NAME> and then enters its context. Use default for <VRF-NAME> to enter the default VRF configure context. Except for the default VRF, the no form of the command deletes the named VRF instance and any IP configuration for interfaces or SVI linked to default VRF. The default VRF cannot be deleted and a warning is given if attempted. To erase the Route-Distinguisher and Route-Targets, enter the default VRF context and delete them manually one by one. Specifies the VRF name. Range: Up to 32 alphanumeric chara cters. The mgmt VRF cannot be used. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command changes the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current subnet. The router uses hello packets to inform neighbor routers of its presence and to comput the hello hold time. Hello hold time informs neighbor routers estimated wait time for the next hello packet from the router. Shortening the hello interval reduces the hello hold time. Default is set to 30 seconds. The no form of this command resets the frequency of PIM hello messages back to the default value on the current subnet. Configures the given value as hello interval. Range: 1-300 seco nds. • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. • Lower values increase PIM Hello packet rate and control-plane / CPU load. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. In chatty networks where there is a chance of Hello packets being lost, configuring a higher hold time reduces the likelihood of adjacency loss. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> command do?

The copy checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> command copies an existing checkpoint configuration to a USB drive. The file format is defined when the checkpoint was created. <CHECKPOINT-NAME> Specifies the name of the checkpoint to copy. The checkpoint n ame can be alphanumeric. It can also contain underscores (_) an d dashes (-). <STORAGE-URL>> The USB drive must be formatted with the FAT file system. Specifies the name of the target file on the USB drive using the following syntax: usb: /<FILE> Copies a remote configuration file to a checkpoint. The remote configuration file must be in checkpoint format. Syntax: copy <REMOTE-URL> checkpoint <CHECKPOINT-NAME> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the policy copy command do?

The policy copy command copies a policy to a new destination policy or overwrites an existing policy. Copying a policy copies all its entries as well. Specifies the policy to be copied. Specifies the name of the destination policy. <POLICY-NAME> <DESTINATION-POLICY> Resequences numbering in a policy. <POLICY-NAME> Specifies the policy where you want to resequence policy entri es. Specifies the sequence number to start resequencing from. Public policy resequence 558 <STARTING-SEQ-NUM> Specifies how much to increment the sequence numbers by. <INCREMENT> Changes the user-specified policy configuration to match the active policy configuration. Use this command when a discrepancy exists between what the user configured and what is active and accepted by the system. Specifies the policy to be reset. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-POLICY>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command configures a gateway address for the DHCP relay agent to use for DHCP requests. By default DHCP relay agent picks the lowest-numbered IP address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the gateway address. Specifies the IP address of the gateway in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax:
- no ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR>
- ip bootp-gateway 713.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. Public ip dns domain-name 837 vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command do?

The ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax:
- ip dns host 838
- no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

syntax:
- ip dns host 838
- no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> command this command creates an ERPS ring with a given ID. The no form of this command removes all the configurations of the ring, including instances. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> This command configures the ERPS ring member port. An L2 interface in the switch is associated to one of the two member ports of an ERPS ring. In case of an interconnection node, only port0 is applicable for the sub-ring. The no form of this command removes the association of the ring port to the L2 interface on the switch. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Public erps ring <RINGID> <port0|port1> interface 859 Required, set port0 of the ring. Required, set port1 of the ring. Required, interface name (string). Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command configures the default revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In revertive operation, after the conditions causing protection switching are cleared, traffic channels are restored to the recovered link blocking the RPL. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. The no form of this command configures non-revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In non-revertive operation, the traffic channels continue to use the RPL, if it has not failed, after conditions causing protection switching are cleared. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> In ERPS, there is a central node called RPL Owner Node which blocks one of the ports to ensure that there is no loop formed for the Ethernet traffic. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command the R-APS messages transmitted by ERPS take the form of OAM PDUs as defined in G.8013. Each OAM PDU is transmitted at a specified level known as the Maintenance Entity Group (MEG) level. This command configures the level with which the ERPS packets must be transmitted. The no form of this command removes the configured MEG level and sets it to the default value of 7. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the meg-level. Range: 0-7. Default: 7. <RINGID> <0-7> This command associates a sub-ring to a parent-ring and is required for the sub-ring to notify the parent- ring on change in topology. The no form of this command removes the parent ring identifier. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the feature-pack mode command do?

The feature-pack mode command set the operation mode for a feature pack deployment. HPE Aruba Networking provides three modes for feature pack management: cloud-managed, file-based, and honor. In the event of a mismatch between the installed feature pack and the feature pack mode, the device will operate in honor mode. cloud-managed file-managed honor no... If the switch is in cloud-managed feature pack mode, use this command to define the switch's feature pack profile. A switch in cloud-managed mode uses the information in this profile to contact the feature pack management server and download and install any allocated feature packs. Public feature-pack server 955 If the subscription pool for the profile contains more than on e subscription block, specify the subscription block within that pool to be assigned. Syntax: feature-pack mode.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the redundancy switchover command do?

The redundancy switchover command causes the switch to immediately switch over to the Standby Management Module. This command must be executed from the Active Management Module and will fail if the Standby Management Module is in a failed state or not present. Syntax: redundancy switchover.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show system high-capacity-tcam command do?

The show system high-capacity-tcam command shows the high capacity TCAM feature current configuration or the pending configuration change (if any) and the current high capacity TCAM feature configuration of each line module plus a reminder to reboot (when needed). The three possible high capacity TCAM feature names are displayed as follows (Ingress IPv4 Port ACL is the default): Ingress IPv4 Port ACL Ingress IPv4 VLAN ACL Routed Ingress IPv4 VLAN ACL Changes the show command output to be in the form of CLI co mmands. Configuring the high capacity TCAM for Ingress IPv4 VLAN ACLs and then showing the configuration before and after reboot. Notice the reminders for the required reboot. Syntax: show system high-capacity-tcam [commands].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip icmp unreachable command do?

The ip icmp unreachable command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages on the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. The unreachable host address originates from the failed packed. Default setting. The no form of this command disables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages from the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. This command does not prevent other hosts from sending an ICMP unreachable message. Syntax: no ip icmp unreachable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. Syntax:
- no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>
- ip igmp apply access-list 1041.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures an IGMP last member query interval value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the value to a default of 1 second on an interface. Public ip igmp last-member-query-interval 1043 Specifies an IGMP last-member-query-interval on the inter face. Default: 1 second. Range: 1-2 seconds. <INTERVAL-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command do?

The ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command configures initial IGMP querier-wait-time value in seconds. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier-wait-time to the default value of 260 seconds. Note that the wait timer can be configured to any numbers within the 1-300 second range. Configures IGMP querier-wait-time to desired value. <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>. Syntax: no ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command configures IGMP robustness on an interface, depending on the command context. The robustness parameter allows tuning for the expected packet loss on a subnet. The no form of this command sets the robustness value to the default of 2 on an interface. Specifies an IGMP robustness value on the interface. Default: 2. Range: 1-7. Public ip igmp robustness 1050. Syntax: no ip igmp robustness <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp router-alert-check command do?

The ip igmp router-alert-check command enables or disables IGMP router alert check for IGMP packets. IGMP packets without the router alert field set are dropped if router alert check is enabled. Router alert check is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables router alert check for IGMP packets. Enable IGMP router alert check. Disable IGMP router alert check. Syntax: ip igmp router-alert-check [enable | disable].

The ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP> command configures an IGMP static multicast group on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. You can configure a maximum of 32 IGMP static groups. The no form of the command unconfigures IGMP static multicast group on an interface. Specifies an IGMP static multicast group IP address on the inter face. Format: A. B. C. D <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>. Syntax: no ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>.

The no form of the command configures the default IGMP version, 3, on the interface. Specifies the IGMP version on the interface. Select 2 for IGMPv 2 (RFC2236). Select 3 for IGMPv3 (RFC3376). Values: 2 or 3. Public ip igmp version 1054. Syntax: no ip igmp version <VERSION>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command do?

The ip igmp snooping all-vlans command enable IGMP snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables IGMP snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command is issued. When IGMP snooping is enabled globally, IGMP snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global IGMP snooping, allows IGMP snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level IGMP snooping settings. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping all-vlans.

The no form of this command deletes a loopback interface. Public interface loopback 1115 Specifies the loopback interface ID. Range: 1 to 256 <INSTANCE>. Syntax: no interface loopback <ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command do?

The interface vlan <VLAN-ID> command creates an interface VLAN also know as an SVI (switched virtual interface) and changes to the config-if- vlan context. The specified VLAN must already be defined on the switch. The no form of this command deletes an interface VLAN. Specifies the loopback interface ID. <VLAN-ID> none. Syntax:

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip tcp mss <VALUE> command do?

The ip tcp mss <VALUE> command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. Public ip tcp mss 1121. Syntax: no ip tcp mss <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show interface statistics command do?

The show interface statistics command shows statistics for switch interfaces such as packets transmitted and received, bytes transmitted and received, broadcast and multicast packets. Specifies a interface name. <IFNAME> <IFRANGE> LAG <LAG-ID> VXLAN <VXLAN-ID> Specifies the port identifier range. Shows LAG interface information. Specifies the LAG number. Range: 1-256 Shows the VXLAN interface information. Specifies the VXLAN interface identifier. Default: 1 Public show interface statistics 1165 Continuously monitor interface statistics. Shows statistics rounded to the nearest power of 1000, for exa mple, 1K, 345M, 2G. Shows only non zero statistics. Displays information about transceivers present in the switch. The information shown varies for different transceiver types and manufacturers. Syntax: show interface [<IFNAME>|<IFRANGE>] statistics [non-zero] [human-readable].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip neighbor-flood command do?

The ip neighbor-flood command enables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface when a neighbor link goes down. The no form of this command disables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface. Displays the status of IP Neighbor Flood on the specified interface along with other interface-related attributes. <IFNAME> Specifies the interface name (for example, vlan30). Optional. Public show ip interface (Neighbor Flood) 1203 Displays the interfaces on which IP Neighbor Flood is enabled. Public show ip neighbor-flood 1204 Displays the current running configuration. Configures HTTP as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Requires destination URL and type of HTTP request (raw/get). The no version of this command disables HTTP as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Syntax: ip neighbor-flood.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command do?

The ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command creates an IP Service Level Agreement (SLA) profile and switches to the config-ip-sla context. The no form of this command deletes an IP-SLA profile. By default, all profile use the default VRF (default). Specifies an IP-SLA profile name. Length: 1 to 64 characters. <IP-SLA-NAME> Selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command do?

The ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command enables the IPv6-only-preferred mode on the VLAN interface, and is also supported on the OOBM interface. Once enabled, the DHCP client prioritizes a IPv6 address over an IPv4 address. To obtain a DHCPv4 address, the IPv6-only-preferred command must be disabled, and the DHCPv4 client must be restarted. This command is disabled by default. NOTE Ensure the server supports the IPv6-only-preferred mode option, as the command will not work unless supported by the server. The no form of this command disables IPv6-only-preferred mode. NOTE The IPv6-only-preferred mode command must be configured prior to the ip dhcp command, otherwise it will not take effect. Syntax: no ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command controls the M fLAGsetting in RAs the router transmits on the current interface. Enable the M flag to indicate that hosts can obtain IP address through DHCPv6. The M flag is disabled by default. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the M flag. 0 0 0 1 Indicates that no information is available via DHCPv6. Indicates that other configuratio n information is available via DH CPv6. Examples of such informat ion are DNS-related informatio n or information on other servers within the network. Public IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag 1287 M flag O flag 1 1 0 1 Indicates that addresses are avail able via Dynamic Host Configura tion Protocol (DHCPv6). If the M flag is set, the O flag is redundant and can be ignored be cause DHCPv6 will return all avai lable configuration information. Syntax: no IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra max-interval command configures the maximum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the default lifetime value. Specifies the maximum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 4-1800. Default: 600 seconds. <TIME> Configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv6 client source binding information to the switch IPv6 binding database. Although DHCPv6 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IPv6 binding database. NOTE Statically configured IPv6 binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv6 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv6 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <IPv6-ADDR> <MAC-ADDR> <IFNAME> Specifies the client IPv6 address. Syntax: no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. Public ip irdp holdtime 1325 The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Syntax: no ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

Specifies the maximum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 4 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 600 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. The no form of this command removes the specified minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements and reverts to the default value. Specifies the minimum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 3 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 450 seconds (75% of the default value for maximum route r advertisement interval). <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface persona lag <NAME> command do?

The interface persona lag <NAME> command creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Aggregation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines configuration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. Only LAG interfaces can reference persona LAG templates. The persona LAG name cannot use reserved interface names or formats, such as vlan, lag, or loopback. Configurations defined in a persona LAG are valid only for LAG interfaces. The no form of this command deletes the persona LAG interface. Specifies the name of the persona LAG. <NAME>. Syntax: no interface persona lag <NAME>.

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command sets an IPv4 address and subnet mask to a LAG interface. One primary and up to 31 secondary address can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Public ip address (interface lag) 1411 Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <MASK> secondary. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary].

Shows LLDP statistics for the OOBM interface. Shows the LLDP TLVs that are configured for send and receive. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level Public aaa authentication login privilege-elevation 1508 access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

command: aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators
syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command do?

The aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command defines authorization as being basic local RBAC (specified as none), or as full-fledged local RBAC specified as local (the default), or as remote TACACS+ (specified with group <GROUP-LIST>). Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually. All server groups named in the command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. The no form of this command unconfigures authorization for the specified connection type, reverting to the default of local. NOTE Although only TACACS+ servers are supported for remote authorization, local authorization (basic or full-fledged) can be used with remote RADIUS authentication. Syntax: no aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping command this command configures the drop unknown mode. While MLD snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that initiate an MLD request for multicast. Drop unknown mode can be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (exclusive-vlan). The default configuration is vlan-shared. The no form of this command configures the drop unknown mode on the switch to the default vlan-shared. Public MLD snooping global configuration commands 1610 Required: Enable shared VLAN filter on the switch. vlan-exclusive Required: Enable exclusive drop unknown filter per VLAN. This command delays the refresh for some IGMP or MLD protocol-related values. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping drop-unknown {vlan-shared | vlan-exclusive}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the MLD join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command disables the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. <ACL-NAME> NOTE If the access list is configured for both L2 VLA N and L3 VLAN, the L3 VLAN configuration will be applied. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

Specifies the number of a single VL AN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by c ommas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the specified ports as fastleave ports. Enables the switch to immediately remove an interface from the bridge table upon receiving the leave group message. The no form of this command disables fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). MLD fastleave is configured for ports on a per-VLAN basis. By default, the querier sends a MLD Group- Specific Query message out of the interface, upon which the leave group message is received to ensure that no other receivers are connected to the interface. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the given ports in forced fastleave mode. The no form of this command disables forced fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a forced fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, sepa Public IPv6 mld snooping forced fastleave vlan 1624 rated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (1 0-40, 60). With forced fastleave enabled, MLD speeds up the process of blocking unnecessary multicast traffic to a switch port that is connected to multiple end nodes. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command this command enables AOS-CX leaf devices (access-layer switches that connect directly to servers, storage, and other endpoints) to unconditionally forward multicast traffic to all VTEPs that do not support the exchange of MLD reports over the fabric. By default, when MLD snooping is enabled in AOS-CX, multicast traffic is selectively forwarded only to VTEPs connected to have interested hosts. This is achieved by exhanging MLD reports over VXLAN tunnels where each VTEP processes the remote reports and uses the membership information to forward multicast traffic efficiently, conserving fabric bandwidth. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip msdp sa-cache command do?

The show ip msdp sa-cache command shows MSDP Peer SA-Cache information for the given VRF. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. The SA-Cache output can be filtered based on the source or group IPv4 address. Shows MSDP SA-Cache information for all VRFs. Optional. Shows MSDP SA-Cache information for a particular VRF. If the <VRF-NAME> is not specified, it shows information for the de fault VRF. Optional. Shows the filtered SA-cache output for the specified source or group IPv4 address. Format: A. B. C. D. Optional. Shows MSDP peer summary for a given VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows the MSDP peer summary for all VRFs. Optional. Shows the MSDP peer summary for a particular VRF. If the <VR F-NAME> is not specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Syntax: show ip msdp sa-cache [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME> | <SRC-OR-GRP-IP>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show spanning-tree mst interface command do?

The show spanning-tree mst interface command shows MSTP configurations for the given instance ID with corresponding port details. Specifies the MSTP instance number. Range: 0 to 4094. <INSTANCE-ID> <IFNAME> vsx-peer Specifies an interface. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Shows spanning tree port summary information. Shows spanning tree root summary information. Enables the spanning tree protocol on the switch. The no form of this command disables the spanning tree protocol on the switch. Enables the bpdu filter for the interface. The BPDU filter feature allows control of spanning tree participation on a per-port basis. Syntax: show spanning-tree mst <INSTANCE-ID> interface <IFNAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command do?

The ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command is optional when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command should not be enabled when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-sparse datapath-auto-include.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip multicast-static-route (group) command do?

The show ip multicast-static-route (group) command displays the multicast static route and corresponding summarized route information for the given group address in the given VRF briefly. If VRF is not specified, the default VRF is displayed. <GROUP-ADDRESS> Specifies the group address. <SOURCE-ADDRESS> Specifies the source address. <ANY> [all vrfs] Selects any source address. Selects all VRFs to display. [vrf <VRF-NAME>] Specifies the VRF to display. The default is default vrf. Displays the multicast static route and corresponding summarized route information for the specified VRF. If VRF is not specified, the default VRF is displayed. Selects all VRFs to display. Public show IPv6 multicast-static-route 1815 Specifies the VRF to display. The default is default vrf. Syntax: show ip multicast-static-route <GROUP-ADDRESS> {<SOURCE-ADDRESS | ANY>}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp enable command do?

The ntp enable command enables the NTP client on the switch to automatically adjust the local time and date on the switch. The NTP client is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables the NTP client. Syntax: no ntp enable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command do?

The ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command sets a key as trusted. When NTP authentication is enabled, the switch only synchronizes with time servers that transmit packets containing a trusted key. The no form of this command removes the trusted designation from a key. Specifies the identification number of the key to set as trusted. Range: 1 to 65534. <KEY-ID>. Syntax: no ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf dead-interval command do?

The ip ospf dead-interval command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead if no hello packet is received on the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPF interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally 4 times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL> Sets the time interval between OSPF hello packets for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the time interval OSPF hello packets to the default of 10 seconds for the OSPF interface. Specifies the time interval for the hello interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 10. <INTERVAL> Sets the key chain for md5 authentication. Syntax: ip ospf dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command do?

The ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command sets the md5 message digest authentication key. If the md5 key is given in ciphertext, it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. The no form of this command deletes the md5 authentication key. Specifies the md5 key ID. Range: 1 to 255. <KEY-ID> {ciphertext | plaintext} Selects the md5 key format. Specifies the md5 authentication key. <KEY> NOTE When the authentication key is not provided on the command line, plaintext key prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered key characters are masked with asterisks. Configures the OSPF network type for the interface. Public ip ospf network 1939 The no form of this command sets the network type for the interface to the system default which is broadcast network. Syntax: ip ospf message-digest-key <KEY-ID> md5 [{ciphertext | plaintext} <KEY>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf shutdown command do?

The ip ospf shutdown command disables OSPF on the interface. The interface state changes to Down. It does not remove the interface from the OSPF area. To remove the interface, use the command no ip ospf area. The no form of this command re-enables OSPF on the interface. Syntax:
- no ip ospf shutdown
- ip ospf shutdown 1946.

syntax:
- no ip ospf shutdown
- ip ospf shutdown 1946

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf transit-delay command do?

The ip ospf transit-delay command sets the time delay in link state transmission for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the delay in link state transmission to the default of 1 second for the OSPF interface. Specifies the transit delay in seconds. Range: 1 to 3600. Default: 1. <DELAY> Sets the key chain for md5 authentication. A key chain configures rotating keys for packet authenticating, reducing the risk of keys being compromised. The no form of this command deletes the key chain used for md5 authentication. Name of key chain to be used for md5 authentication. <KEYCHAIN-NAME> Sets the protocol to advertise a maximum metric so that other routers do not prefer this router as an intermediate hop in their shortest path first (SPF) calculations. Syntax: ip ospf transit-delay <DELAY>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router ospf <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The router ospf <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command creates an OSPF process (if not created already) on a VRF, and switches to the OSPF router instance context. Up to eight OSPF processes are supported per VRF. The no form of this command removes the OSPF instance. Public router ospf 1962 Specifies an OSPF process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name for the OSPF process. Default: default. Syntax: no router ospf <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router-id (ospf) command do?

The router-id (ospf) command sets an ID for the router in an IPv4 address format. The no form of this command unconfigures the router-id for the instance and sets the router-id to the default as follows: the router-id is selected dynamically as equal to the highest loopback address on the router, or the highest active interface if there are no loopback addresses. If no IP address is configured on any interfaces on the router, OSPF will not form an adjacency. Specifies the Router ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <ROUTER-ADDR> Sets the SHA (secure hash authentication) key for the selected virtual link. If the SHA key is given in ciphertext, it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. Syntax: router-id <ROUTER-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip ospf neighbors command do?

The show ip ospf neighbors command displays information about OSPF neighbors. <PROCESS-ID> Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. neighbors <NEIGHBOR-ID> Select to display information about a particular neighbor, specifi ed in IPv4 format (A. B. C. D). interface <INTERFACE-NAME> Select to display neighbor information only for the specified in terface. detail summary all-vrfs Select to display detailed information for all the neighbors. Select to display summary information for the neighbors. Select to display neighbor information for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Displays OSPF routing information. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] neighbors [<NEIGHBOR-ID>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command configures a static OSPFv3 neighbor on the interface for point-to-multipoint networks. Static neighbors are required on non-broadcast networks where automatic neighbor discovery using multicast hello packets is not possible. The neighbor IPv6 address must be the link-local IPv6 address of the neighboring router on the same link. This command is valid only on interfaces configured with the point-to-multipoint network type. It is required for non-broadcast point-to-multipoint networks. The no version of this command removes the static neighbor configuration from the interface. neighbor <neighbor-IPv6- Static neighbor IPv6 link-local address. address>. Syntax: no IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol <AREA-ID> Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. Public IPv6 ospfv3 area 2045. Syntax: no IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <area-id>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null command configures null authentication on an interface which disables IPsec authentication. Configures IPSec AH authentication. OSPFv3 interfaces that have IPsec configured at the interface context will not use area level IPsec. The no form of this command removes IPsec AH authentication for the specified area. Specifies the Security Parameters Index (SPI) to use. The SPI is an identification tag carried in the IPsec AH header. It enables t he receiving OSPF process to select and use the Security Association (SA) from the SA table. The SPI must be unique on the sw itch. Range: 256 to 4294967295. Specifies the authentication type: md5 or sha1. Specifies the key type to use: plaintext (unencrypted), hex-st ring (encrypted) or ciphertext (encrypted). Specifies the authentication key. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval command sets the interval after a neighbor is declared dead when no hello packet is received on the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPFv3 interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally four times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL> Configures IPsec ESP authentication. OSPFv3 interfaces that have IPsec configured at the interface context will not use area level IPsec ESP. The no form of this command removes IPsec ESP for the specified area. spi <SPI-INDEX> <AUTH-TYPE> <KEY-TYPE> Specifies the Security Parameters Index (SPI) to use. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3-af retransmit-interval command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3-af retransmit-interval command sets the time between retransmitting lost link state advertisements for the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the time between retransmitting lost link state advertisements to the default 5 seconds. Specifies the time interval for the retransmit interval, in seconds. Range: 1 to 3600. Default: 5 <seconds>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3-af retransmit-interval <seconds>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 ospfv3 routes command do?

The show IPv6 ospfv3 routes command shows the OSPFv3 routing table information. <PROCESS-ID> <PREFIX/LENGTH> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies an OSPFv3 process ID that shows information from th e OSPFv3 routing table for the particular OSPFv3 process. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Specifies the IPv6 destination prefix showing information about a particular destination prefix. For example, 2010: bd9: : /32. Select to show OSPFv3 routing table information for all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Shows OSPFv3 statistics. Specifies an OSPFv3 process ID that shows information on the OSPFv3 SPF statistics for the particular OSPFv3 process. Rang e: 1 to 65535. Public show IPv6 ospfv3 statistics 2093 Select to show OSPFv3 SPF statistics information for all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Syntax: show IPv6 ospfv3 [<PROCESS-ID>] routes [<PREFIX/LENGTH>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command do?

The ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. If not configured, the implementation derives the value from the current hello-interval. Syntax: ip pim-dense hello-holdtime <holdtime-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip mroute group-addr command do?

The show ip mroute group-addr command shows the multicast routing information for the given group address. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <GROUP-ADDR> <SOURCE-ADDR> Specifies a group address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies show information for the group from this source in IP v 4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. all-vrfs Shows mroute information for the group for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. vsx-peer Shows brief version of the multicast routing information. Optionally, you can specify the display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows mroute information for all VRFs. Syntax: show ip mroute <GROUP-ADDR> [<SOURCE-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <vrf-name>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense IPv6-addr command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense IPv6-addr command enables the router to dynamically determine the source IP address to use for PIM packets sent from the interface or to use the specific IP address. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of any. Specifies an IPv6 address as the source IP for the interface. <IPv6-ADDR-VALUE> any. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense IPv6-addr {<IPv6-ADDR-VALUE> | any}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense override-interval command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense override-interval command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Default: 2500 ms. Range: 500-6000. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Default: 500 ms. R ange: 250-2000. <DELAY-VALUE> Configures the multicast datagram time-to-live (router hop-count) threshold for the interface. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. The default is enabled. With LAN-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors how long it will wait before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. Other downstream routers on the same interface must send a join to override the prune before the LAN-prune-delay time to continue the flow. Prompts any downstream neighbors with multicast receivers continuing to belong to the flow to reply with a join. If no joins are received after the LAN-prune-delay period, the router prunes the flow. The propagation-delay and override-interval settings determine the LAN-prune-delay setting. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay 2573 The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay.

The IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Range: 500 to 6000. Default: 2500. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Range: 250 to 200 0. Default: 500. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse propagation-delay 2576 Configures the frequency at which the router will send periodic join or prune-interval messages. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 pim6 pending command do?

The show IPv6 pim6 pending command shows the pending joins on a PIM router. Optionally you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Use this command to determine what flows are being requested on the PIM network. If data availability for a flow is expected, and a join for the flow is pending, the troubleshooting search moves to the source of that flow, since the routers are verified to be seeing the request for data. <GROUP-ADDR> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Specifies a group address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxx x: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number fro m 0 to F. Shows information for all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show IPv6 pim6 pending [<GROUP-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command configures an application to associate with a user-configured certificate. By default, all applications are associated with the default certificate "local-cert". Note that applications "captive-portal" and "hsc" are only available on certain platforms. The certificate associated with EST client is global to all EST profiles. Among the available applications, only dot1x-supplicant, est-client, and radsec-client are allowed to associate with device-identity certificate. Also note that device-identity certificate on a switch using a TPM of legacy version 1.2 only works with TLS version 1.2. 1. Syntax: no crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. Specifies the name of a leaf certificate. Range: 1 to 32 alphanu meric characters (excluding "). <CERT-NAME>. Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command do?

The radius-server timeout [<1-60>] command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RADIUS server before trying the next RADIUS server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Specifies the timeout interval of 1 to 60 seconds. Default: 5 sec onds. Syntax: no radius-server timeout [<1-60>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the tacacs-server key command do?

The tacacs-server key command creates or modifies a TACACS+ global passkey. The TACACS+ global passkey is used as a shared-secret for encrypting the communication between all TACACS+ servers and the switch. The TACACS+ global passkey is required for authentication unless local passkeys have been set. By default, the TACACS+ global passkey is empty. If the administrator has not set this key, the switch will not be able to perform TACACS+ authentication. The switch will instead rely on the authentication mechanism configured with aaa authentication login. NOTE When this command is entered without parameters, plaintext passkey prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered passkey characters are masked with asterisks. The no form of the command removes the global passkey. Syntax: tacacs-server key [plaintext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY> | ciphertext <GLOBAL-PASSKEY>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command creates RIP process if not already created and enters the router rip <PROCESS-ID> context for the VRF mentioned. If no VRF is mentioned, a default is used. Only one RIP process is allowed per VRF. The no form of this command deletes the RIP instance for the VRF. If no VRF is mentioned the default is deleted. Specifies name of the RIP process ID. Range: 1-63. <PROCESS-ID> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies VRF name. Syntax: no router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear ip rip statistics command do?

The clear ip rip statistics command clears RIP event statistics. Specifies RIP process ID. Range: 1-63 Clears statistics for all VRFs. Selects VRF to clear statistics for. Specifies VRF name. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> Enables RIP process for RIP enabled IP address configured on interface. The no form of this command disables RIP process on interface. Public RIP v2 interface commands 2956 Disables RIP process for RIP enabled IP addresses configured on interface. The no form of this command enables RIP process on interface. Disables an interface from sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. The no form of this command enables interface for sending RIP packets for a specific IP address. Disables interface from receiving RIP packets for a specific IP address. Syntax: clear ip rip [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>] command associates an SNMP MIB view with the SNMP community. The no form of this command removes the associated SNMP MIB view from the SNMP community. Public snmp-server community view 3201 Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Specifies the view name for the SNMP MIB view. Accepts a maxi mum of 32 characters. <STRING> <VIEW-NAME>. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING> [view <VIEW-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community <STRING> command do?

The snmp-server community <STRING> command adds an SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. A community string is like a password that controls read/ write access to the SNMP agent. A network management program must supply this name when attempting to get SNMP information from the switch. A maximum of 10 community strings are supported. Once you create your own community string, the default community string (public) is deleted. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. When no community string exists, a default community string with the value public is automatically defined. Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Syntax: no snmp-server community <STRING>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server snmpv3-only command do?

The snmp-server snmpv3-only command accepts SNMP v3 messages only, SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c will be disabled. By default SNMP v1, SNMP v2c and SNMP v3 will all be enabled. The no form of this command restores the default setting and reenables SNMP v1 and SNMP v2c. Syntax:
- no snmp-server snmpv3-only
- snmp-server snmpv3-only 3227.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ssh ciphers command do?

The ssh ciphers command configures SSH to use a set of ciphers in the specified priority order. Ciphers in SSH are used for privacy of data being transported over the connection. The first cipher type entered in the CLI is considered a first priority. Each option is an algorithm that is used to encrypt the link and each name indicates the algorithm and cryptographic parameters that are used. Only ciphers that are entered by the user are configured. The no form of this command removes the configuration of ciphers and reverts SSH to use the default set of ciphers. Syntax: ssh ciphers <CIPHERS-LIST>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ssh server vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The ssh server vrf <VRF-NAME> command enables the SSH server on the specified VRF. SSH is disabled by default and will not be operational till the admin password is set on the switch. Note that the admin password is considered set even if it is configured to be empty. The no form of the command disables the SSH server on the specified VRF. If no VRF is specified, by default the SSH server will be enabled on the default or mgmt VRF, depending on the switch model. Public ssh server vrf 3280 Specifies the VRF name. Syntax: no ssh server vrf <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf <VRF-NAME> [name <WORD>] command do?

The vrf <VRF-NAME> [name <WORD>] command adds the destination IPv4 static route on the specified VRF. If no <VRF-NAME> is specified the route is applied to the default VRF. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 static route from the VRF. <DEST-IPv6-ADDR> <MASK> <NEXT-HOP-IP-ADDR> <INTERFACE> nullroute reject Specifies the route destination in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 128. Specifies the next hop in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a deci mal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the next hop as an outgoing interface. Silently discards packets to the destined route. Discards packets to the destined route and returns an ICMP er ror to the sender. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Syntax: vrf <VRF-NAME> [name <WORD>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command do?

The ssh certificate-as-authorized-key command enables SSH enforcement that the username must be present in the certificate that is being used for authorization. This configuration alters how certificate-based authentication maps to a user account. When this is enabled, SSH will not require local user association with an authorized-key and instead enforces that the username used to log in is present within the certificate. The SSH server will check for the username in certificate fields Common Name or User Principle Name for a match. If a certificate is not used for authentication then this configuration has no effect on SSH authentication. The no form of this command disables the SSH enforcement of username in the certificate. Syntax: no ssh certificate-as-authorized-key.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show-tech ufd command do?

The show-tech ufd command executes the show ufd command followed by the show running-config ufd command. Shows information on all UFD sessions or the specified UFD session. Specifies an existing UFD session ID. Range: 1 to 128. Public show ufd 3522 Enables UFD (Uplink Failure Detection). UFD is disabled by default. This command must be issued before the configuration that is set with related UFD commands takes effect. The no form of this command disables UFD. Creates the specified UFD (Uplink Failure Detection) session and then enters its context. If the specified session already exists, this command enters its context. The no form of this command deletes the specified session configuration. Specifies the UFD session ID. Range: 1 to 128. Public ufd session-id 3525. Syntax: show-tech ufd.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip forward-protocol udp command do?

The ip forward-protocol udp command defines the UDP server to which the interface forwards ingress UDP broadcast packets received on a specific UDP port. A maximum of 8 UDP broadcast servers can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes traffic forwarding for the specified server and port/protocol. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies the UDP server IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), wh ere x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the UDP port number for which traffic is forwarded. Public UDP commands 3527 Specifies the protocol name for which traffic is forwarded. Syntax: ip forward-protocol udp <IPv4-ADDR> {<PORT-NUM> | <PROTOCOL>}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan <VLAN-LIST> command do?

The vlan <VLAN-LIST> command creates a VLAN and changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. By default, the VLAN is enabled. To disable a VLAN, use the shutdown command. If the specified VLAN exists, this command changes to the config-vlan-id context for the VLAN. If a range of VLANs is specified, the context does not change. NOTE VLANs used for internal purposes using the command system internal vlan range cannot be used for any other (L2) purposes. The no form of this command removes a VLAN. VLAN 1 is the default VLAN and cannot be deleted. Specifies a single ID, or a series of IDs separated by commas (2, 3, 4), dashes (2-4), or both (2-4, 6). Range: 1 to 4094. Public vlan (interface) 3583 Creates an access interface and assigns an VLAN ID to it. Only one VLAN ID can be assigned to each access interface. Syntax: vlan access <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan protocol <PROTOCOL_NAME> command do?

The vlan protocol <PROTOCOL_NAME> command adds protocol mapping to a VLAN on an interface. The no form of this command removes protocol mapping from the VLAN on an interface. Specifies a VLAN ID. Range: 2 to 4094. <VLAN-ID> <PROTOCOL_NAME>. Syntax: no vlan protocol <PROTOCOL_NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan translate <VLAN-1> command do?

The vlan translate <VLAN-1> command defines a bidirectional VLAN translation rule that maps an original VLAN ID (VLAN-1) to a translated internal VLAN ID (VLAN-2) on a LAG or layer 2 interface. Applies to both incoming and outgoing traffic. On the HPE Aruba Networking 8400 Switch Series: Along with translated and native VLANs, VLAN trunk allowed VLANs are also respected and traffic is allowed. Public vlan translate 3588 The no form of this command removes an existing VLAN translation rule on the current interface. NOTE VLAN translation and MVRP cannot be enabled on the same interface. A translated VLAN must be present on the switch before the rule is created; the original VLAN need not be present. Specifies the number of an origin VLAN. Range: 1 - 4094. <VLAN-1> <VLAN-2>. Syntax: no vlan translate <VLAN-1>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vlan trunk allowed command do?

The vlan trunk allowed command assigns a VLAN ID to an trunk interface. Multiple VLAN IDs can be assigned to a trunk interface. These VLAN IDs define which VLAN traffic is allowed across the trunk interface. VLANs can only be assigned to non-routed (Layer 2) interfaces. All interfaces are non-routed (Layer 2) by default when created. Use routing and no routing commands to move ports between Layer 3 and Layer 2 interfaces. The no form of this command removes one or more VLAN IDs from a trunk interface. When the last VLAN is removed from a trunk interface, the interface continues to operate in trunk mode, and will trunk all the VLANs currently defined on the switch, and any new VLANs defined in the future. To disable the trunk interface, use the command shutdown. Syntax: vlan trunk allowed [<VLAN-LIST> | all].
