messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx shutdown-on-split command do?

The vsx shutdown-on-split command shuts down the configured non-VSX interfaces on the VSX secondary along with VSX interfaces during a VSX split. The no form of this command resumes the non-VSX interfaces that are shut down during the VSX split. NOTE This command has no effect on the VSX primary during a split. However, when applied on the VSX primary, the command will bring down the non-VSX interfaces until linkup delay timer expires during the VSX primary reboot. Syntax: no vsx shutdown-on-split.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command do?

The vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command enables VSX sync of active gateways or policies associated under an interface. To synchronize the associations, you must configure the same interface vlan on the peer switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization for active gateways or policies associated under an interface, but it does not remove the feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. {[active-gateways] [policies]} access-gateways policies. Syntax: no vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync arp-security command do?

The vsx-sync arp-security command enables VSX synchronization of the ARP security configurations on the primary VSX switch to the secondary peer switch. After you enter vsx-sync arp-security, you must enter vsx-sync mclag-int erfaces for enabling VSX synchronization for the ARP security feature. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of ARP security configurations on VLAN mode and LAG interface mode to the secondary peer switch. However, it does not remove the existing ARP security configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync arp-security.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync bfd-global command do?

The vsx-sync bfd-global command enables syncing of global BFD configurations, such as echo-src-ip-address, detect-multip lier, min-transmit-interval, and min-receive-interval, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. NOTE This command enables VSX synchronization only at the top level and not at the context level. The no form of this command disables the syncing of global BFD configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the existing global BFD feature configurations from it. Syntax: no vsx-sync bfd-global.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync dhcp-server command do?

The vsx-sync dhcp-server command enables VSX synchronization of all DHCPv4 server configurations, including external storage configurations, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. Only the primary VSX node answers DHCP service requests, and leases can only be exported from the primary VSX node. The no form of the command disables VSX synchronization of DHCPv4 server configurations to the secondary peer; however, it does not remove the existing DHCPv4 server feature configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync dhcp-server.

To synchronize DHCP snooping configurations associated with a particular VLAN and interface, configure the same VLAN and interface on the peer device. The no form of this command disables syncing DHCP snooping configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync dhcp-snooping.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync loop-protect-global command do?

The vsx-sync loop-protect-global command enables the VSX synchronization of global loop protect configurations, such as transmit-interval and re-enable-timer, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. To enable VSX synchronization at the context level for this feature, enter the vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command at the context level. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global loop protect configurations, but it does not remove the existing global loop protect feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync loop-protect-global.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command do?

The vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command enables the VSX synchronization of VSX LAG interface associations and attributes on the primary VSX switch to the secondary peer switch. The Usage section in this topic provides a listing of specific associations and attributes that are synchronized to the secondary switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global VSX LAG and attributes, but it does not remove the existing VSX LAG feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync mclag-interfaces.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync nd-snooping command do?

The vsx-sync nd-snooping command enables VSX synchronization of ND snooping configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. To synchronize ND snooping configurations associated with a particular VLAN and interface, configure the same VLAN and interface on the peer device. When RA guard policy is enabled, this command also synchronizes RA guard policy related configurations. The no form of this command disables syncing ND snooping configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync nd-snooping.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync neighbor command do?

The vsx-sync neighbor command enables VSX synchronization of IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configuration on primary VSX node to the secondary peer. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. If any new modification or additional configuration is made on the primary node for IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configuration, they will be auto-synced. The no form of this command VSX synchronization of IPv4 and IPv6 static neighbors configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync neighbor.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync ospf command do?

The vsx-sync ospf command enables syncing of OSPF (including OSPFv2 and OPSFv3), route map, and key chain configurations on the primary VSX switch. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. To synchronize OSPF configurations at the port level context, configure the same port on the peer device. The no form of this command disables syncing of OSPF, route map, and key chain configurations to the secondary peer. But it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. NOTE The OSPF router ID is not synchronized. This exclusion is needed because the router ID uniquely identifies the router. The two OSPF routers with the same router ID do not form an adjacency between them. Syntax:
- no vsx-sync ospf
- vsx-sync ospf 3763.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync qos-global command do?

The vsx-sync qos-global command enables the VSX synchronization of global QoS configurations, such as CoS map, DSCP map, and trust policy, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. To enable VSX synchronization at the context level for this feature, enter the vsx-sync qos command at the context level. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global QoS configurations, but it does not remove the existing global QoS feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync qos-global.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync route-map command do?

The vsx-sync route-map command enables syncing of all As Path lists, community lists, prefix lists, and route map configurations on primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. There is no configuration sync from the secondary to primary peer. The no form of this command disables syncing of As Path lists, community lists, prefix lists, and route map configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. NOTE When vsx-sync for BGP or OSPF is configured, route map configurations are synchronized from primary VSX node to the secondary peer due to the dependency in configurations. Syntax:
- no vsx-sync route-map
- vsx-sync route-map 3767.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync stp-global command do?

The vsx-sync stp-global command enables the VSX synchronization of global STP configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. Use the vsx-sync mclag-interfaces command to sync context level spanning trees. To enable VSX synchronization at the context level for this feature, enter the vsx-sync mclag-inte rfaces command at the context level. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global STP configurations, but it does not remove the existing global STP feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync stp-global.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync telnet command do?

The vsx-sync telnet command enables VSX synchronization of Telnet server configurations from the primary VSX node to the secondary peer. To synchronize Telnet configurations associated with a particular VRF, you need to configure the same VRF on the peer device. The no form of the command disables VSX synchronization of Telnet server configurations to the secondary peer, however, it does not remove the existing Telnet server configurations from the secondary peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync telnet.

The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization of global time-related configurations, but it does not remove the existing global time-related feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: no vsx-sync time.

The no form of the command disables the VSX synchronization of UDP forwarder configurations to the secondary peer; however, it does not remove the existing udp-forwarder configurations from the secondary VSX peer. Syntax: no vsx-sync udp-forwarder.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vsx-sync vrrp command do?

The vsx-sync vrrp command enables VSX synchronization of all VRRP configurations on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. There is no configuration sync from secondary to primary peer. To synchronize VRRP configurations at the port level context, the same port must be configured on the peer device with IP address. Public vsx-sync vrrp 3779 The no form of this command disables syncing VRRP configurations to the secondary peer, but it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer. NOTE BFD IP is the IP address of VRRP peer device. Hence it cannot be synced. In the owner scenario, in case the priority is synced, both VSX primary and secondary devices will have 255 as their priority. Syntax: no vsx-sync vrrp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erase all zeroize command do?

The erase all zeroize command restores the switch to its factory default configuration. You will be prompted before the procedure starts. Once complete, the switch will restart from the primary image with factory default settings. Syntax: erase [all] zeroize.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server authentication password command do?

The https-server authentication password command enables authentication using username and password, which corresponds to the default authentication mechanism. Enabling the password authentication mode disables the certificate authentication mode. NOTE Only one authentication method can be enabled at a time. Syntax: https-server authentication password.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server max-user-sessions command do?

The https-server max-user-sessions command sets the maximum amount of concurrent open sessions for any given user through the HTTPS server. The amount of concurrent open sessions may have an impact on system performance, so it is recommended to set this value to the minimum necessary. Specifies the maximum number of user sessions allowed. Defaul t: 6. Maximum value: 8. <SESSION-AMT>. Syntax: https-server max-user-sessions <SESSION-AMT>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the https-server max-user-sessions command?

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server rest access-mode command do?

The https-server rest access-mode command changes the REST API access mode. The default mode is read-write. This command does not affect Central connections, which have permission to alter configurations regardless of the access mode set on the switch. Selects the read/write mode. Allows POST, PUT, PATCH, and DE LETE methods to be called on all configurable elements in the s witch database. Selects the read-only mode. Write access to most switch resou rces through the REST API is disabled. Public https-server rest access-mode 1025. Syntax: https-server rest access-mode {read-only | read-write}.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the https-server rest access-mode command?
The syntax of the https-server rest access-mode command is: https-server rest access-mode {read-only | read-write}.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server session close all command do?

The https-server session close all command invalidates and closes all HTTPS sessions. All existing WebUI sessions (including sessions used for Central connections) will be logged out. REST and WebUI users will have to reauthenticate. and all real-time notification feature Web Socket connections are closed and must be resubscribed. Syntax: https-server session close all.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the https-server session close all command?
The syntax of the https-server session close all command is: https-server session close all.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server session-timeout command do?

The https-server session-timeout command configures the timeout, in minutes, for any given HTTPS server session. A value of 0 disables the timeout. This command does not affect sessions used for Central connections. <MINUTES> Specifies the maximum idle time, in minutes for an HTTPS sess ion. Default: 20. Maximum: 480 (8 hours). 0 disables the timeou t, but the maxium is still enforced. Public https-server session-timeout 1029. Syntax: https-server session-timeout <MINUTES>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the https-server session-timeout command?
The syntax of the https-server session-timeout command is: https-server session-timeout <MINUTES>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the https-server vrf command do?

The https-server vrf command configures and starts the HTTPS server on the specified VRF, allowing access to REST and the WebUI from ports assigned to that VRF. This command does not affect access to HPE Aruba Networking Central instances, as this feature has its own dedicated connection channel. The no form of the command stops any HTTPS servers running on the specified VRF and removes the HTTPS server configuration. Public https-server vrf 1030 Specifies the VRF name. Required. Length: Up to 32 alpha num eric characters. By using this command, you enable access to both the Web UI and to the REST API on the specified VRF. You can enable access on multiple VRFs. By default, HPE Aruba Networking 8100, 8320, 8325, 8360, 8400, 9300/9300S, 10000, 10040 Switch Series have an HTTPS server enabled on the mgmt VRF. Syntax: https-server vrf <VRF-NAME>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the https-server vrf command?
The syntax of the https-server vrf command is: https-server vrf <VRF-NAME>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp apply access-list command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. Syntax: ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip igmp apply access-list command?
The syntax of the ip igmp apply access-list command is: ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp last-member-query-interval command do?

The ip igmp last-member-query-interval command configures an IGMP last member query interval value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the value to a default of 1 second on an interface. Public ip igmp last-member-query-interval 1043 Specifies an IGMP last-member-query-interval on the inter face. Default: 1 second. Range: 1-2 seconds. <INTERVAL-VALUE>. Syntax: ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip igmp last-member-query-interval command?
The syntax of the ip igmp last-member-query-interval command is: ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp querier interval command do?

The ip igmp querier interval command configures the interval between IGMP queries on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier interval to the default value of 125 seconds on an interface. Specifies the IGMP querier interval in seconds on the interface. Default: 125 seconds. Range: 5-300. <INTERVAL-VALUE>. Syntax: ip igmp querier interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp querier query-max-response-time command do?

The ip igmp querier query-max-response-time command configures the IGMP querier max response time value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the querier max response time value to the default of 10 seconds on an interface. Specifies the IGMP querier max response time value on the inte rface. Default: 10 seconds. Range: 10-128 seconds. <RESPONSE-TIME>. Syntax: ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp robustness command do?

The ip igmp robustness command configures IGMP robustness on an interface, depending on the command context. The robustness parameter allows tuning for the expected packet loss on a subnet. The no form of this command sets the robustness value to the default of 2 on an interface. Specifies an IGMP robustness value on the interface. Default: 2. Range: 1-7. Public ip igmp robustness 1050. Syntax: ip igmp robustness <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp static-group command do?

The ip igmp static-group command configures an IGMP static multicast group on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. You can configure a maximum of 32 IGMP static groups. The no form of the command unconfigures IGMP static multicast group on an interface. Specifies an IGMP static multicast group IP address on the inter face. Format: A. B. C. D <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>. Syntax: ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp version strict command do?

The ip igmp version strict command configures an IGMP strict version on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. Drops packets that do not match the configured version. The no form of the command removes the strict version configuration from the interface. Specifies the IGMP version on the interface. Select 2 for IGMPv 2 (RFC2236). Select 3 for IGMPv3 (RFC3376). Values: 2 or 3. <VERSION>. Syntax: ip igmp version <VERSION> strict.

The no ip igmp command disables all IGMP configurations on an interface or sub-interface, depending on the command context you are in. Syntax: no ip igmp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping (config mode) command do?

The ip igmp snooping (config mode) command configures drop-unknown and fastlearn modes on the ports. While IGMP snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that made an IGMP request for the multicast. Drop unknown filters ensure that packets are not forwarded to ports that did not make a request for the traffic stream. This could either be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (vlan-exclusive). The default is vlan-shared. Fast learn enables the port to learn group information when receiving a topology change notification. By default, fast learn is not enabled on ports. Drop unknown filters ensure that packets are not forwarded to ports that did not make a request for the traffic stream. Enables a shared VLAN filter on the switch. Default is vlan-sh ared. Syntax: ip igmp snooping.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the ip igmp snooping (config mode) command is: ip igmp snooping.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping (interface mode) command do?

The ip igmp snooping (interface mode) command configure IP IGMP snooping for the VLAN on the interface. When IGMP snooping is enabled, the L2 snooping switch forwards multicast packets of known multicast groups to only the receivers. When IGMP snooping is not enabled, the snooping switch floods multicast packets to all hosts on the VLAN. Public ip igmp snooping (interface mode) 1086 auto vlan <VLAN-LIST> blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST> fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST> forced-fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST> Instruct the device to monitor incoming multicast traffic on the specified ports on a VLAN or VLAN range. This is the default behavior. Enter the number of a single VLAN or a series of num bers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 4 0), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). Syntax: ip igmp snooping.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface (port) command do?

The interface (port) command switches to the config-if context for a physical port. This is where you define the configuration settings for the logical interface associated with the physical port. Specifies a physical port number. Format: member/slot/port. <PORT-NUM> Public interface (port) 1114. Syntax: interface <PORT-NUM>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface loopback command do?

The interface loopback command creates a loopback interface and changes to the config-loopback-if context. Loopback interfaces are layer 3. The no form of this command deletes a loopback interface. Public interface loopback 1115 Specifies the loopback interface ID. Range: 1 to 256 <INSTANCE>. Syntax: interface loopback <ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface vlan command do?

The interface vlan command creates an interface VLAN also know as an SVI (switched virtual interface) and changes to the config-if- vlan context. The specified VLAN must already be defined on the switch. The no form of this command deletes an interface VLAN. Specifies the loopback interface ID. <VLAN-ID> none. Syntax: interface vlan <VLAN-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address (interface) command do?

The ip address (interface) command sets an IPv4 address for the current layer 3 interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 128. Specifies a secondary IP address. (Supported on 6300, except for S3L75A, S3L76A, S3L77A.) <IP-ADDR> <MASK> secondary. Syntax: ip address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK>.

The syntax of the ip address (interface) command is: ip address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK>

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip mtu command do?

The ip mtu command sets the IP MTU (maximum transmission unit) for an interface. This defines the largest IP packet that can be sent or received by the interface. This value should be less than or equal to the overall MTU for the interface. The no form of this command sets the IP MTU to the default value 1500. Specifies the IP MTU in bytes. Range: 68 to 9198. Default: 150 0. <VALUE>. Syntax: ip mtu <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip tcp mss command do?

The ip tcp mss command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. Public ip tcp mss 1121. Syntax: ip tcp mss <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show interface transceiver command do?

The show interface transceiver command displays information about transceivers present in the switch. The information shown varies for different transceiver types and manufacturers. Only basic information is shown for unsupported HPE and third-party transceivers installed in the switch and they are also identified with an asterisk in the output. Specifies the name or range of an interface on the switch. Use t he format member/slot/port (for example, 1/3/1). <INTERFACE-ID> detail Show detailed information for the interfaces. threshold-violations Show threshold violations for transceivers. vsx-peer. Syntax: show interface [<INTERFACE-ID>] transceiver [detail | threshold-violations].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show interface supported-transceivers command do?

The show interface supported-transceivers command displays transceivers supported by a specific, or all, interface(s) in the switch. Information varies and is dependent on transceiver types, part number, and short description or interfaces. Public show interface supported-transceivers 1174 Specifies the name of an interface on the switch. Use the following format: member/slot/port. Syntax: show interface {<IFNAME>|<IFRANGE>} supported-transceivers.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla command do?

The ip-sla command creates an IP Service Level Agreement (SLA) profile and switches to the config-ip-sla context. The no form of this command deletes an IP-SLA profile. By default, all profile use the default VRF (default). Specifies an IP-SLA profile name. Length: 1 to 64 characters. <IP-SLA-NAME>. Syntax: ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the ip-sla command is: ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla responder command do?

The ip-sla responder command selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Specifies the port number to listen for IP-SLA probes. Range: 1 to 65535. <PORT-NUM> Public ip-sla responder 1215 source {<SOURCE-IPv4- ADDR>|<SOURCE-IPv6-ADDR>| <IFNAME>} Selects the source IPv4 or IPv6 address for SLA probes or the s ource interface to use for sending IP-SLA probes. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address (tunnel) command do?

The ip address (tunnel) command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip address (tunnel) command?
The syntax of the ip address (tunnel) command is: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address (tunnel) command do?

The IPv6 address (tunnel) command sets the local IP address of an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel or of an IPv6 to IPv6 tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel. <IPv6-ADDR> <MASK> Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal numbe r from 0 to F. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Public IPv6 address (tunnel) 1242. Syntax: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address (tunnel) command?
The syntax of the IPv6 address (tunnel) command is: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip mtu (tunnels) command do?

The ip mtu (tunnels) command sets the MTU (maximum transmission unit) for an IP interface. The default value is 1500 bytes. The no form of this command sets the MTU to the default value of 1500 bytes. Public ip mtu (tunnels) 1243 Specifies the MTU in bytes. Range: 1, 280 bytes to 9, 192 bytes. <VALUE>. Syntax: ip mtu <VALUE>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip mtu (tunnels) command?
The syntax of the ip mtu (tunnels) command is: ip mtu <VALUE>

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address link-local command do?

The IPv6 address link-local command enables IPv6 on the current interface. If no address is specified, an IPv6 link-local address is auto-generated for the interface. If an address is specified, auto-configuration is disabled and the specified address/mask is assigned to the interface. To disable IPv6 link-local on the interface, remove IPv6 address link-local, IPv6 address <global-IPv6- address>, and IPv6 address autoconfig from the interface. NOTE This feature is not available on the management VRF. Public IPv6 address link-local 1273 Specifies the IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zero s (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hextet o f four zeros to a single 0. Syntax: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>].

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address link-local command?
The syntax of the IPv6 address link-local command is: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>]

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd cache-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd cache-limit command configures the limit on the number of neighbor entries in the ND cache. The no form of this command sets the cache limit to the default value. Specifies the neighbor cache entries limit. Range: 1-131072. Default: 131072. <CACHELIMIT>. Syntax: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd cache-limit command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd cache-limit command is: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd dad attempts command do?

The IPv6 nd dad attempts command configures the number of neighbor solicitations to be sent when performing duplicate address detection (DAD) for a unicast address configured on an interface. If the active gateway is configured with the same IP as an SVI IP, then IPv6 DAD cannot be configured. The no form of this command sets the number of attempts to the default value. dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS> Specifies the number of neighbor solicitations to send. Range: 0 -15. Default: 1. Syntax: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd dad attempts command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd dad attempts command is: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd hop-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd hop-limit command configures the hop limit to be sent in RAs. The no form of this command resets the hop limit to 0. This reset eliminates the hop limit from the RAs that originate on the interface, so the host determines the hop limit. hop-limit <HOPLIMIT> Specifies the hop limit. Range: 0-255. Default: 64. Syntax: IPv6 nd hop-limit <HOPLIMIT>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd hop-limit command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd hop-limit command is: IPv6 nd hop-limit <HOPLIMIT>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd mtu command do?

The IPv6 nd mtu command configures the MTU size to be sent in the RA messages. The no form of this command sets hop limit to the default value. <MTU-VALUE> Specifies the MTU size. Range: 1280-65535 bytes. Default: 1 500 bytes. Public IPv6 nd mtu 1278. Syntax: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the IPv6 nd mtu command is: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ns-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ns-interval command configures the ND time in milliseconds between DAD neighbor solicitations sent for an unresolved destination. Increase the ns-interval time if the network is slow or if there are persistent retry failures. If the active gateway is configured with the same IP as an SVI IP, then IPv6 DAD cannot be configured The no form of this command sets the ns-interval to the default value. Public IPv6 nd ns-interval 1279 Specifies the neighbor solicitation interval. Range: 1000-3600 000 milliseconds. Default: 1000 milliseconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the IPv6 nd ns-interval command is: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd prefix command do?

The IPv6 nd prefix command specifies prefixes for the routing switch to include in RAs transmitted on the interface. IPv6 hosts use the prefixes in RAs to autoconfigure themselves with global unicast addresses. The autoconfigured address of a host is composed of the advertised prefix and the interface identifier in the current link-local address of the host. By default, advertise, autoconfig, and onlink are set. The no form of this command removes the configuration on the interface. Specifies the IPv6 prefix to advertise in RA. Format: X: X: : X: X/M <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN> default Specifies apply configuration to all on-link prefixes that are n ot individually set by the IPv6 ra prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PRE FIX-LEN> command. Syntax: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd prefix command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd prefix command is: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command do?

The IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command configures the DNS Search List (DNSSL) to include in Router Advertisements (RAs) transmitted on the interface. The no form of this command removes the DNS Search List from the RAs transmitted on the interface. Specifies the domain names for DNS queries. <DOMAIN-NAME> lifetime <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>].

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command is: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>]

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra dns server command do?

The IPv6 nd ra dns server command configures the IPv6 address of a preferred Recursive DNS Server (RDNSS) to be included in Router Advertisements (RAs) transmitted on the interface. The no form of this command removes the configured DNS server from the RAs transmitted on the interface. <IPv6-ADDR> Specifies the RDNSS address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zeros (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hex tet of four zeros to a single 0. For example, this address 2222: 0 000: 3333: 0000: 0000: 0000: 4444: 0055 becomes 2222: 0: 333 3: : 4444: 55. lifetime <TIME> Specifies IPv6 DNS server lifetime in seconds. Range: 4-4294 967295 seconds. Default: 1800 seconds. Public IPv6 nd ra dns server 1284. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns server <IPv6-ADDR> [lifetime <TIME>].

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns server command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns server command is: IPv6 nd ra dns server <IPv6-ADDR> [lifetime <TIME>]

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command do?

The IPv6 nd ra lifetime command configures the lifetime, in seconds, for the routing switch to be used as a default router by hosts on the current interface. The no form of this command sets lifetime to the default of 1800 seconds. Specifies lifetime in seconds of a default router. A setting of 0 fo r default router lifetime in an RA indicates that the routing switc h is not a default router on the interface. Range: 0-9000 seco nds. Default: 1800 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command is: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra min-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra min-interval command configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the current max-interval setting. Specifies a minimum advertisement time in seconds. Range: 3 -1350. Default: 200 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra min-interval <TIME>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra min-interval command is: IPv6 nd ra min-interval <TIME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command do?

The IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command sets the amount of time that the interface considers a device to be reachable after receiving a reachability confirmation from the device. The no form of this command sets the reachable time to the default value of 0. (no limit). Public IPv6 nd ra reachable-time 1292 Specifies the reachable time in milliseconds. Range: 1000-36 00000. Default: 0 (no limit). <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command is: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command do?

The IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command configures the period (retransmit timer) between ND solicitations sent by a host for an unresolved destination, or between DAD neighbor solicitation requests. By default, hosts on the interface use their own locally configured NS-interval settings instead of using the value received in the RAs. Increase this timer when neighbor solicitation retries or failures are occur, or in a "slow" (WAN) network. The no form of this command sets the value to the default of 0. Specifies the retransmit timer value in milliseconds. Range: 0 - 4294967295 milliseconds. Default: 0 (Use locally configured N S-interval). <TIME>. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command is: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd router-preference command?
The syntax of the IPv6 nd router-preference command is: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip route interface command do?

The ip route interface command creates a route leak between the SRC-VRF and DST-VRF. Using the static method, the route must first be added to the destination VRF. The route is added to the local interface of the source VRF with a next-hop interface. The existing IP route command can then take either the next-hop IP or the next-hop interface. • INTERFACE refers to the outgoing interface in an m/s/p format • Users must provide both the next-hop IP and the interface information to leak network routes. Public ip route interface 1341 • The next-hop IP information is not required to leak connected routes. The [no] form of command deletes the static VRF leaked route. Required: IPv4 IP-Address route destination. Syntax: ip route <IPv4 ADDR/MEMBER> interface <IPv4 ADDR>.

document_title: Command Line Interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the ip route interface command?
The syntax of the ip route interface command is: ip route <IPv4 ADDR/MEMBER> interface <IPv4 ADDR>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router bgp vrf command do?

The router bgp vrf command this command configures VRF for the BGP instance. The no form of this command removes the configuration. Specifies a 4-byte AS number in the range 1-4294967295 i n integer format or from 0.1-65535.65535 in dotted format. String VRF name for the VRF. AS-NUMBER <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: router bgp <AS-NUMBER> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the router bgp vrf command is: router bgp <AS-NUMBER> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show bgp info vrf command do?

The show bgp info vrf command displays BGP route-targets information for specified VRF. Display BGP RT information. Required string VRF name for the vrf. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show bgp info vrf <vrf-name>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show job command do?

The show job command shows information about a specific job or every job. Optionally shows the job execution output log. Specifies an existing job name. When omitted, information is sh own for every job. Range: 1 to 64 characters (alphanumeric and "_" (underscore)). Selects the job execution output instance with 1 selecting the most recent. To see the maximum number of job execution out put instances for your particular switch, use command show ca pacities job. Syntax: show job [<JOB-NAME>] [execution-output <INSTANCE-ID>].

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show capacities (job, schedule) command do?

The show capacities (job, schedule) command shows either job or schedule capacities information for your switch model. Public show capacities (job, schedule) 1380. Syntax: show capacities {job | schedule}.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface lag command do?

The interface lag command creates a Link Aggregation Group (LAG) interface represented by an ID. The no form of this command deletes a LAG interface represented by an ID. Specifies a LAG interface ID. <ID>. Syntax: interface lag <ID>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

The syntax of the interface lag command is: interface lag <ID>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address (interface lag) command do?

The ip address (interface lag) command sets an IPv4 address and subnet mask to a LAG interface. One primary and up to 31 secondary address can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Public ip address (interface lag) 1411 Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <MASK> secondary. Syntax: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary].

The syntax of the ip address (interface lag) command is: ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary]

The show interface persona command shows all interface personas and their attached interfaces. Syntax: show interface persona.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show lacp aggregates (LAG) command do?

The show lacp aggregates (LAG) command displays all LACP aggregate information configured for all LAGs, or for a specific LAG. Optional: Specifies a lag name. <LAG-NAME> Public show lacp aggregates (LAG) 1431 Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show lacp aggregates [<LAG-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show lacp interfaces command do?

The show lacp interfaces command displays an LACP configuration of the physical interfaces, including VSX s. If an interface name is passed as argument, it only displays an LACP configuration of a specified interface. Optional: Specifies an interface name. <IFNAME> vsx-peer Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Public show lacp interfaces 1434. Syntax: show lacp interfaces [<IFNAME>] [vsx-peer].

The syntax of the show lag command is: show lag <LAG-ID>

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear lldp neighbors command do?
The clear lldp neighbors command clears all LLDP neighbor details. Syntax: clear lldp neighbors.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear lldp statistics command do?

The clear lldp statistics command clears all LLDP neighbor statistics. Enables LLDP support globally on all active interfaces. By default, LLDP is enabled. The no form of this command disables LLDP support globally on all active interfaces. It does not remove any LLDP configuration settings. Syntax: clear lldp statistics.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication console-login-attempts command do?

The aaa authentication console-login-attempts command for the console interface (channel) only, enables console login attempt limiting. If the number of failed console login attempts equals the configured threshold, the user is locked out for the configured duration. The no form of this command disables console login attempt limits. NOTE If you enable the lockout using this command and also enable the SSH, REST, and Telnet lockout using command aaa authentication limit-login-attempts, and then enter too many consecutive wrong passwords, you may become locked out, and will have to wait for the configured lockout time to elapse before logging in on any interface. NOTE This console login attempt limiting feature is only available when not using remote authentication through AAA servers (TACACS+ or RADIUS) on any interface. Syntax: aaa authentication console-login-attempts <ATTEMPTS> console-lockout-time.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login (local) command do?

The aaa authentication login (local) command defines authentication as being local (with the name local) (the default). Or defines a sequence of remote AAA server groups to be accessed for authentication purposes. Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually as either local or using remote AAA server groups. All server groups named in your command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. Local is always available for any connection type not configured for remote AAA authentication. Public aaa authentication login (local) 1506 The no form of this command removes for the specified connection type, any defined remote AAA server group authentication sequence. Syntax: aaa authentication login <CONNECTION-TYPE> {local | group <GROUP-LIST>}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level Public aaa authentication login privilege-elevation 1508 access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

syntax: aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication minimum-password-length command do?

The aaa authentication minimum-password-length command enables minimum password length checking. Existing passwords shorter than the minimum length are unaffected. Length checking does not apply to ciphertext passwords. Length checking applies both to local and remote authentication. The no form of this command disables minimum password length checking. Specifies the minimum password length. Range: 1 to 32. <LENGTH>. Syntax: aaa authentication minimum-password-length <LENGTH>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authorization commands (local) command do?

The aaa authorization commands (local) command defines authorization as being basic local RBAC (specified as none), or as full-fledged local RBAC specified as local (the default), or as remote TACACS+ (specified with group <GROUP-LIST>). Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually. All server groups named in the command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. The no form of this command unconfigures authorization for the specified connection type, reverting to the default of local. NOTE Although only TACACS+ servers are supported for remote authorization, local authorization (basic or full-fledged) can be used with remote RADIUS authentication. Syntax: aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> {local | none}.

If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Public show aaa authentication (local) 1515. Syntax: show aaa authentication [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear mac-address-table command do?

The clear mac-address-table command this command is used to clear the MAC move count and move history for a single MAC address or VLAN, or for a range of VLANs. If no specific MAC address or VLAN is specified, this command clears statistics for all MAC addresses. Public clear mac-address-table 1556 (Optional) Clear information for a specific MAC address. (Optional) Clear move information for specific VLAN. Syntax: clear mac-address-table.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear mirror command do?

The clear mirror command clears the mirror statistics for all configured mirror sessions or a specified session Specifies all configured sessions. Specifies a numeric identifier for the session. Range: 1 to 4 Specifies a comment for the mirroring session. The no form of this command removes the comment. A comment string of up to 64 characters composed of letters, n umbers, underscores, dashes, spaces, and periods. Public comment 1583. Syntax: clear mirror [all | <SESSION-ID>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy tshark-pcap command do?

The copy tshark-pcap command copies the tshark capture data to a file on a TFTP or SFTP server. <REMOTE-URL> Specifies the capture file on a remote TFTP or SFTP server. The URL format is: {tftp:// | sftp://<USER>@} {<IP>|<HOST>} [: <PORT>] [; blocksi ze=<SIZE>]/<FILE> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. The command causes the mirror session to transmit mirrored packets to the switch CPU. This destination may be configured for multiple sessions, however only one such configured session may be active at a given time. The diagnostic utility Tshark may be used to view and capture packets transmitted to the CPU through this route. Ctrl+C must be entered to terminate a Tshark capture session. More details can be found in the Supportability Guide. Syntax: copy tshark-pcap <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the MLD join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command disables the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. <ACL-NAME> NOTE If the access list is configured for both L2 VLA N and L3 VLAN, the L3 VLAN configuration will be applied. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping auto vlan command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping auto vlan command this command configures the given ports in auto mode, which is the default port mode. The no form of this command disables auto ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as an auto port. Specifies the number of a single VL AN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by c ommas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld robustness (interface) command do?

The IPv6 mld robustness (interface) command this command configures MLD robustness. The robustness value represents the number of times the querier retries queries on the connected subnets. The default robustness-value is 2 seconds. Public IPv6 mld robustness (interface) 1666 robustness-value Required: 1-7, configures MLD robustness. NOTE Default robustness-value is 2 seconds. Use the no IPv6 mld robustnes s command to set robustness-value to the default. Syntax: IPv6 mld robustness <value>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure CoPP?

To configure CoPP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure the default CoPP policy, edit an existing policy, or create a policy with the command copp-p 2. Add, edit, or remove classes in the policy with the command class 3. Review the CoPP policy configuration settings with the command show copp-policy.

document_title: Copp Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/CoPP Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply copp-policy command do?

The apply copp-policy command applies a CoPP policy to the switch, replacing the policy that is in effect. There may be a brief interruption in traffic flow to the management processor while the switch implements the change. Enter the no apply copp-policy <NAME> command with the name of a CoPP policy to unapply a CoPP policy and apply the default CoPP policy. This will only take effect if the specified policy is actively applied. Public apply copp-policy 13 Since there must always be a CoPP policy applied, this command effectively attempts to replace the applied CoPP policy with the default CoPP policy. The default CoPP policy cannot be unapplied using this command. Specifies the name of the policy to apply. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <NAME> default. Syntax: apply copp-policy { <NAME> | default }.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the diag event-trap-disable command do?

The diag event-trap-disable command prevents the specific event notifications from being sent as traps to the SNMP management stations. The no form of this command disables this feature and reenables the notification of events to be sent as traps. Specify up to five comma separated Event IDs To modify the traps to be disabled, such as changing the existing list of event IDs or removing an event ID from the blocked trap list, first completely remove previously configured disabled traps using the CLI command no diag event-trap-disable <EVENT-ID>. After that, reconfigure the new event IDs or set of event IDs for the changes to take effect. The command to disable traps cannot be issued via network management systems such as SNMP, the REST API, HPE Aruba Networking Fabric Composer or HPE Aruba Networking Central. Syntax: diag event-trap-disable <EVENT-IDS>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about IPv6 Example?

IPv6 Example: switch(config)# logrotate target tftp://2001: db8: 0: 1: : 128 Remote transfer of rotated log files Only the TFTP protocol is supported for remote transfer, and both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses are supported. Only newly rotated log files are transferred to the remote host during the log rotation. Previously rotated log files are not transferred. After a file is successfully transferred, it is removed from the switch local path. Packet level failures with TFTP are handled in the protocol itself. With each TFTP session failure, TFTP retries the file transfer three times. Retries have a timeout of five seconds.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE> command do?

The logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE> command specifies the maximum allowed log file size. A log file that exceeds either the logrotate maxsize or the logrotate period (whichever happens first), triggers rotation of the log file. The no form of this command resets the size of the log file to the default (100 MB). <MAX-SIZE> Specifies the allowed size the log file can reach before it is com pressed and stored locally or transferred to a remote host. Rang e: 10 to 200 MB. Default: 100 MB. Syntax: logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE>.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

The syntax of the logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE> command is: logrotate maxsize <MAX-SIZE>

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly} command do?

The logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly} command sets the log file rotation time period. Defaults to daily. A log file that exceeds either the logrotate maxsize or the logrotate period (whichever happens first), triggers rotation of the log file. The no form of this command resets the log rotation period to the default of daily. Rotates log files on a daily basis (default) at 0: 01. Rotates log files every hour at the first second of the hour. Rotates log files monthly on the first day of the month at 00: 01. Rotates log files once a week on Sunday at 00: 01. Setting a weekly period: switch(config)# logrotate period weekly Resetting the period to its default of daily: switch(config)# no logrotate period. Syntax: logrotate period {daily | hourly | monthly | weekly}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Redundant Management communication?

Redundant Management communication: timeout The standby management module has taken over from an un responsive active management module. Redundant Management election timeout A failure to elect a standby management module in the alloted t ime. Critical service fault (error) A daemon critical to switch operation has stopped functioning. An extra error string may be present to describe the error in d etail.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about VSX software update?

VSX software update: Reset triggered by a VSX software update. Chassis critical temperature Chassis operating temperature exceeded. Chassis insufficient fans Insufficient fans to cool the chassis. Chassis unsupported PSUs/fans Unsupported or misconfigured PSUs or system fans. Management module critical temperature Management module operating temperature exceeded. Uncontrolled reboots • ops-switchd crashed • ovsdb-server crashed • Reset Public Reboot reasons 41 ◦ Software thermal reset ◦ Power on reset ◦ Watchdog reset ◦ CPU request reset ◦ cold reset ◦ Long press reset ◦ Jumper reset • switchd_agent crashed Reboot reasons for line module • Line module reboot • Line module crashed Event Logs Event logging logs events generated by daemons, processes, and plug-ins running within the switch software. The event logging framework captures the event logs in a system journal by updating the journal fields and meta data. Subtopics Showing and clearing events Showing and clearing events The clear events command is used to clear the event log of all events.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the split [<COUNT>][<SPEED>][confirm] command do?

The split [<COUNT>][<SPEED>][confirm] command splits a port into multiple interfaces. Only ports capable of supporting breakout cables or SR4/eSR4/eDR4 optics can be split. Specifies the number of child interfaces to activate upon splittin g the port. Default: 4. <COUNT> <SPEED> confirm 8400X 8p 40G QSFP+ Adv Modul e 8400X 6p 40G/100G QSFP28 Ad. Syntax: split [<COUNT>][<SPEED>][confirm].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy checkpoint command do?

The copy checkpoint command copies the checkpoint using TFTP, SFTP, SCP, or USB. Specifies the checkpoint name. <CHECKPOINT-NAME> {<STORAGE-URL> | <REMOTE- URL>} Select either the storage URL or the remote URL for the destin ation of the copied command output. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you troubleshoot?

To troubleshoot, follow the documented procedure: 1. Modify the ACL to allow the ping traffic 2. Unapply the ACL from egress (8400/8320/8325/9300/9300S switches) or Control Plane 3. Ping a destination which is not matched by the ACL. For example, if the ACL is blocking traffic based. Commands shown in the procedure include: PING 100.1.2.10 (100.1.2.10) 100(128) bytes of data; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted; ping: sendmsg: Operation not permitted.

document_title: Diagnostics And Supportability Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Diagnostics and Supportability Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about 1/1 Ingress Global Policy Lookup?

1/1 Ingress Global Policy Lookup: Ingress TCAM Entries 35 2048 Total Ingress TCAM Entries 35 2048 18432 Egress TCAM Entries 0 0 8192 Ingress Lookups 1 8 Ingress Flex Lookups 0 1 Egress Lookups 0 4 Ingress Policers 0 2047 Egress Policers 0 2047 Hardware resources will be consumed on each module that has a policy applied to an interface. Ingress and egress policies consume separate hardware resources. The following example shows the output of a show resources command issued on a 6300 switch with a policy applied to a physical interface in both in and out directions: 6300(config)# show resources Resource Usage: Public Using classifier policies for traffic capture and... 104 Mod Description Resource Used Reserved Free ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- --.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear accounting-logs command do?

The clear accounting-logs command use this command to clear accounting logs. Once issued, only logs generated after this command is run will be displayed in the output of the show accounting log commands. NOTE This command will not clear logs when the logging accounting-format-native feature is configured. To clear accounting logs on switches with this feature enabled, users should first revert the native accounting format back to the default AOS-CX format by executing the no logging accounting-format-native command. Syntax: clear accounting-logs.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the logging command do?

The logging command enables syslog forwarding to a remote syslog server. The no form of this command disables syslog forwarding to a remote syslog server. Public logging 112 Starting with AOS-CX 10.11, payload information is present in accounting logs. The maximum REST payload that can be sent to RADIUS/TACACS server is 1024 characters, and the maximum of REST payload that can be sent to syslog server is 3500 characters. If this limit is is reached, the log will display three dots (...) to indicate that the log an exceeded the character limit and is incomplete. {<IPv4-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR> | <HOSTNAME>} Selects the IPv4 address, IPv6 address, or host name of the rem ote syslog server. Required. Syntax: logging {<IPv4-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR> | <FQDN | HOSTNAME>} {udp [<PORT-NUM>]}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the logging filter command do?

The logging filter command creates a filter to restrict what event or debug logs are logged. A filter can be used to either permit or deny: • The event logs from being generated on the switch, or • The event or debug logs generated on the switch from being forwarded to a syslog server. A filter is identified by a filter name and can have up to 20 rules or entries, each with a different sequence number, matching criteria, and corresponding action (deny or permit). When a filter is applied on a log, the log is matched against the criteria mentioned in the rules or entries in ascending numerical order of their sequence numbers until a matching entry is found. Once a matching entry is found, its corresponding action is applied on the log. If no matching rule is found, the default action (permit) is applied. Syntax: logging filter <FILTER-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about reboot - Reboots the Management Module?

reboot - Reboots the Management Module: password - Set the admin account password secure-mode - Sets or retrieves the secure mode setting sh - Launch support shell umount - Unmounts a storage device update - Update a product image version - Prints ServiceOS release version information cat - Prints files to stdout cp - Copy files and directories du - Estimate file space usage ls - List directory contents md5sum - Compute and check md5 message digest mkdir - Make directories mv - Move (rename) files rm - Remove files or directories rmdir - Remove empty directories tftp - Allows transfer of files to/from a remote machine exit - Logout Enter '<command> --help' for more info Service OS CLI features and limitations The Service OS CLI provides basic shell functionality that allows you to execute commands and pass arguments to those commands only.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip command do?

The ip command shows or configures the port with a static IP address (IPv4 only) or enables the DHCP client on the port. An address is set only if a DHCP server is available to provide one. {show | dhcp | disable | addr <ADDR- NETMASK-GATEWAY>} show dhcp Selects the options for the OOBM port. Shows the OOBM port. Configures the port with a DHCP address. disable Disables the OOBM port. addr <ADDR-NETMASK- GATEWAY> Configures the port with a static IP address (IPv4 only). Specify address, netmask, and gateway as A. B. C. D. Syntax: ip {show | dhcp | disable | addr <ADDR-NETMASK-GATEWAY>}.

Shows output similar to -a, but excludes a period (.) and a dou ble period (..). Shows output list by columns. Shows output list by lines. Shows listing of directory entries instead of contents Public ls 160 Follows symlinks. Follows symlinks on the command line. Recurse. Appends a slash (/) to directory entries. Appends an indicator to entries. An indicator can be as an aster isk (*) or slash (/) or equal sign (=) or at sign (@) or pipe (|). Shows the output in a long listing format. Shows the list inode numbers. Shows a list of numeric UIDs and GIDs instead of names. Shows a list of allocated blocks. Syntax: ls [<OPTIONS>] [<FILE-NME>].
