messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the system serviceos password-prompt command do?

The system serviceos password-prompt command use this command to enable password authentication for ServiceOS. By default, the ServiceOS shell (accessible only from the local switch console port) requires no password to login as an admin use. When this setting is enabled, the same password used to authenticate the admin user in the AOS-CX CLI or WebUI can be used to log in to the ServiceOS shell. If this setting is enabled, a forgotten admin user password cannot be reset using ServiceOS; if there are no other local or RADIUS/TACACS user accounts with administrator-level access, the switch must be zeroized by entering the username zeroize command at the ServiceOS login prompt to restore administrator access. serviceos password-prompt cannot be enabled if the built-in admin user has been disabled. Syntax: system serviceos password-prompt.

The tftp {-b | -g | - - <LOCAL-FILE> | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} host [<PORT>] command transfers files to and from a remote machine (TFTP a file). Public tftp 178 {-b | -g | -l | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} Selects the options for transferring a file. -b -g -l -p Specifies the transfer blocks of size octets. The default blocksi ze is set to 1468, which can be overridden with the -b option. Specifies to get a file. Specifies a local file. Specifies to put a file in remote location. -r <REMOTE-FILE> Specifies a remote file. Specifies the port for transfer. If no port option is specified, TFT P uses the standard UDP port 69 by default. <PORT>. Syntax: tftp {-b | -g | - - <LOCAL-FILE> | -p | -r <REMOTE-FILE>} host [<PORT>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Initial Configuration?

Initial Configuration: Perform the initial configuration of a factory default switch using one of the following methods: • Load a switch configuration using zero-touch provisioning (ZTP). When ZTP is used, the configuration is loaded from a server automatically when the switch booted from the factory default configuration. Public SNMP-based management support 21 • Connect to the switch wirelessly with a mobile device through Bluetooth, and use the Aruba CX Mobile App to deploy an initial configuration from a provided template. The template you choose during the deployment process determines how the management interface is configured. Optionally, as the final deployment step, you can select to import the switch into Net Edit through a WiFI connection to the Net Edit server. Alternatively, you can use the Aruba CX Mobile App to manually configure switch settings and features for a subset of the features you can configure using the CLI. You can also access the CLI through the mobile application.

To configure in-band management on a data port, follow the documented procedure: 1. Use an Ethernet cable to connect a data port to your network 2. Configure a layer 3 interface on the data port 3. Enable SSH support on the interface (on the default VRF) with the command ssh server vrf d 4. Enable the Web UI on the interface (on the default VRF) with the command https-server vrf default.

section: Profile switching between VSX and VSF
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Profile switching between VSX and VSF?

Profile switching between VSX and VSF: • VSF and VSX are mutually exclusive features. Before switching between profiles, ensure the device is in standalone mode. • Configuration data is not compatible between VSF and VSX profiles. Back up your configurations before changing profiles (switching will erase startup configurations and reboot the device). • Never apply the VSX profile on an active VSF setup. Applying VSX profile on an active VSF commander may cause undesired behavior and potentially require a factory reset. • Checkpoints created in one profile mode should not be applied to a switch in a different profile mode. For example, do not apply VSX checkpoints on a switch configured for VSF profile, or vice versa. • Configuration downloads should match the current profile of the switch. Do not download VSX configurations to a switch in VSF profile mode, or vice versa. Software downgrade considerations • When performing a software downgrade on a device configured with either VSX or VSF profiles, ensure that the startup configuration is erased prior to the downgrade process.

topic: Profile switching between VSX and VSF

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip static command do?

The ip static command assigns an IPv4 or IPv6 address to the management interface. The no form of this command removes the IP address from the management interface and sets the interface to operate as a DHCP client. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the number of bits in an IPv4 or IPv6 address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32 for I Pv4, and 0 to 128 for IPv6. <IP-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: ip static <IP-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp authentication-key command do?

The ntp authentication-key command defines an authentication key that is used to secure the exchange with an NTP time server. This command provides protection against accidentally synchronizing to a time source that is not trusted. The no form of this command removes the authentication key. NOTE Using "#" in the NTP password will prevent the NTP to syncronize when authentication is enabled Specifies the authentication key ID. Range: 1 to 65534. <KEY-ID> md5 sha1 <PLAINTXT-KEY> trusted ciphertext <ENCRYPTED-KEY> Selects MD5 key encryption. Specifies SHA1 key encryption. Specifies the plaintext authentication key. Range: 8 to 40 chara cters. The key may contain printable ASCII characters excluding "#" or be entered in hex. Keys longer than 20 characters are ass umed to be hex. Syntax: ntp authentication-key <KEY-ID> {md5 | sha1}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp server command do?

The ntp server command defines an NTP server to use for time synchronization, or updates the settings of an existing server with new values. Up to eight servers can be defined. Public ntp server 52 The no form of this command removes a configured NTP server. NOTE The default NTP version is 4; it is backwards compatible with version 3. server <IP-ADDR> key <KEY-NUM> minpoll <MIN-NUM> maxpoll <MAX-NUM> burst iburst prefer Specifies the address of an NTP server as a DNS name, an IPv4 address (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, o r an IPv6 address (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxx x), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. When specifying an IPv4 address, you can remove leading zero s. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.16 8.5.100. Syntax: ntp server <IP-ADDR> [key <KEY-NUM>] [minpoll <MIN-NUM>] [maxpoll <MAX-NUM>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ntp associations command do?

The show ntp associations command shows the status of the connection to each NTP server. The following information is displayed for each server: • Tally code: The first character is the Tally code: ◦ (blank): No state information available (e.g. non-responding server) ◦ x: Out of tolerance (discarded by intersection algorithm) ◦.: Discarded by table overflow (not used) Public show ntp associations 57 ◦ -: Out of tolerance (discarded by the cluster algorithm) ◦ +: Good and a preferred remote peer or server (included by the combine algorithm) ◦ #: Good remote peer or server, but not utilized (ready as a backup source) ◦ *: Remote peer or server presently used as a primary reference ◦ o: PPS peer (when the prefer peer is valid) • ID: Server number. Syntax: show ntp associations [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the flow-control rx command do?

The flow-control rx command command flow-control enables negotiation of receive flow control on the current interface. The switch advertises RX support to the link partner. The final configuration is determined based on the capabilities of both partners. Command flow-control priority enables priority-based flow control (PFC). A single priority is supported.. Each invocation of this command replaces the previous configuration. The no form of these commands disables any configured flow control on the selected interface. Enables the ability to cease and resume transmission when rece iving IEEE 802.3x LLFC pause frames on this interface. Specifies the packet priority for which PFC will operate. Range: 0 to 7. One priority can be specified. Syntax: flow-control rx.

Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 128. Specifies a secondary IP address. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the l3-counters [rx | tx] command do?

The l3-counters [rx | tx] command enables counters on a layer 3 interface. By default, all interfaces are layer 3. To change a layer 2 interface to layer 3, use the routing command. The no form of this command, with no specification, disables both transmit and receive counters on a layer 3 interface. To disable transmit (tx) or receive (rx) counters only, specify the counter type you want to disable. rx tx. Syntax: l3-counters [rx | tx].

On the 10000 Switch Series, “access” persona ports are typically connected to workloads / VMs, and the “uplink” (fabric) persona ports are connected to the core / spine. The no form of this command removes the interface persona. Selects the access persona type. Selects the uplink persona type. custom <PERSONA-NAME> Selects the custom persona type with a user-provided name. Range: 1 to 64 printable ASCII characters including space. copy attach. Syntax: persona {access | uplink | custom <PERSONA-NAME>} [copy | attach].

Shows additional statistics, including the tx filtered and rx filte red counters. • Rx filter packets are protocol packets received when the p rotocol is disabled on the switch and there is only one port i n the VLAN. Protocols include OSPF, PIM, RIP, LACP, and LL DP. • An example of a Tx filtered packet would be a multicast pac ket being filtered from going out of the ingress port. Shows statistics rounded to the nearest power of 1000, for exa mple, 1K, 345M, 2G. This is available only in the CLI interface o utput. Shows only non zero statistics. Shows LAG interface information. Continuously monitor interface statistics. Shows loopback interface information.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show interface dom command do?

DOM information also consists of vendor determined thresholds which trigger high/low alarms and warning flags. Specifies an interface. Format: member/slot/port. <INTERFACE-ID> detail vsx-peer Show detailed information. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about VLANs?

VLANs: are primarily used to provide network segmentation at layer 2. VLANs enable the grouping of users by logical function instead of physical location. They make managing bandwidth usage within networks possible by: • Allowing grouping of high-bandwidth users on low-traffic segments Public Source interface selection 166 • Organizing users from different LAN segments according to their need for common resources and individual protocols • Improving traffic control at the edge of networks by separating traffic of different protocol types. • Enhancing network security by creating subnets to control in-band access to specific network resources VLANs are generally assigned on an organizational basis rather than on a physical basis. For example, a network administrator could assign all workstations and servers used by a particular workgroup to the same VLAN, regardless of their physical locations. Hosts in the same VLAN can directly communicate with one another. A router or a Layer 3 switch is required for hosts in different VLANs to communicate with one another.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the checkpoint rename command do?

The checkpoint rename command renames an existing checkpoint. Public checkpoint rename 183 <OLD-CHECKPOINT-NAME> Specifies the name of an existing checkpoint to be renamed. <NEW-CHECKPOINT-NAME> Specifies the new name for the checkpoint. The checkpoint nam e can be alphanumeric. It can also contain underscores (_) and dashes (-). NOTE Do not start the checkpoint name with CPC be cause it is used for system-generated checkpo ints. Syntax: checkpoint rename <OLD-CHECKPOINT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy {primary secondary} <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The copy {primary secondary} <REMOTE-URL> command uploads a firmware image to a TFTP or SFTP server. {primary | secondary} Selects the primary or secondary image profile to upload. Requi red <REMOTE-URL> TFTP format: Specifies the URL to receive the uploaded firmware using SFTP, TFTP or SCP. tftp://<IP-ADDR>[: <PORT-NUM>] [; blocksize=<Value>]/<FILENAME> SFTP format: sftp://<USERNAME>@<IP-ADDR> [: <PORT-NUM>]/<FILENAME> SCP format: scp://USER@{IP|HOST}[: PORT]/FILE vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Public copy {primary | secondary} <REMOTE-URL> 229. Syntax: copy {primary | secondary} <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the hot-patch apply|remove <name.patch> command do?

The hot-patch apply|remove <name.patch> command apply hot-patch software or remove it from the switch. The no form of the hot-patch apply command disables the hot-patch image, but does not remove it from the switch. Rebooting the system after disabling or removing the patch is not required. Profile names apply <name.patch> remove <name.patch> Apply the specified hot-patch image to a standalone switch or VSF stack. AOS-CX hot-patch software images can be obtaine d from HPE Aruba Networking customer support, and are identif ied with a.patch extension. Disables the hot-patch image and removes the patch from the switch. This removal will also disable the patch. Once removed, a hot-patch must be downloaded again in order to be applied. Public hot-patch 239. Syntax: hot-patch apply|remove <name.patch>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure the DNS client?

To configure the DNS client, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure one or more DNS name servers with the command ip dns server 2. To resolve DNS requests by appending a domain name to the requests, either configure a single domain 3. To use static name resolution for certain hosts, associate an IP address to a host with the command ip 4. Review your DNS configuration settings with the command show ip dns. Commands shown in the procedure include: VRF Name: vrf_mgmt; VRF Name: vrf_default; VRF Name: mainvrf.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns domain-name command do?

The ip dns domain-name command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. Public ip dns domain-name 249 The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <DOMAIN-NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what restrictions or notes are documented for Guidelines?

For Guidelines, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: • An LLDP must not contain more than one EEE TLV. • LLDP may or may not be enabled on the interfaces supporting EEE. If LLDP is not enabled, it might not be in the optimal operational mode. • EEE TLV should not be exchanged until it negotiates from L1 and it detects peer EEE capabilities. • EEE TLV is disabled by default and enabled only when EEE is active. • EEE and EEE TLV exchange can be enabled or disabled at the interface level.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lldp dot3 {poe | macphy} command do?

The lldp dot3 {poe | macphy} command sets the 802.3 TLVs to be advertised. By default, advertisement of both POE and MAC/PHY TLVs is enabled. Not supported on the OOBM interface. The no form of this command disables advertisement of 802.3 TLVs. Specifies advertisement of power over Ethernet data link classif ication. Specifies advertisement of media access control and physical l ayer information. Enabling advertisement of the POE TLV: switch(config-if)# lldp dot3 poe Disabling advertisement of the POE TLV: switch(config-if)# no lldp dot3 poe. Syntax: lldp dot3 {poe | macphy}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lldp holdtime-multiplier <multiplier> command do?

The lldp holdtime-multiplier <multiplier> command sets the holdtime TTL multiplier value that is used to calculate the LLDP Time-to-Live value. Time-to-Live defines the length of time that neighbors consider LLDP information sent by this agent as valid. When Time-to-Live expires, the information is deleted by the neighbor. Time-to-live is calculated by multiplying holdtime by the value of lldp timer. The no form of this command sets the holdtime TTL multiplier to its default value of 4. Specifies the TTL multiplier in the range of 2 to 10. Default: 4. <multiplier> Formula TTL = Holdtime-multiplier x lldp timer where: TTL = Time-to-Live Holdtime-multiplier = Multiplying holdtime value lldp timer = Message transmission interval. Syntax: lldp holdtime-multiplier <multiplier>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no lldp management-address vlan <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no lldp management-address vlan <VLAN-ID> command sets the VLAN whose IPv4 or IPv6 address is advertised as the LLDP management authority. The no form of this command removes the VLAN whose IPv4 or IPv6 address is advertised as the LLDP management authority. The following is the precedence for the management IP address TLV in the LLDP packet (in order): • LLDP management-IP-address and management-IPv6-address, if configured. • LLDP management VLAN's IPv4 and IPv6 address, if configured. • Loopback IP address from the smallest configured loopback interface identifier. • Route-only-port IP address (Layer-3 interface) or IP address of the SVI (Layer-2 interface). • OOBM IP address. • Base MAC address of the switch. Specifies the VLAN ID. Range 1-4094.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lldp med [poe [priority-override] | capability | network-policy] command do?

The lldp med [poe [priority-override] | capability | network-policy] command configures support for the LLDP-MED TLV. LLDP-MED (media endpoint devices) is an extension to LLDP developed by TIA to support interoperability between VoIP endpoint devices and other networking end- devices. The switch only sends the LLDP MED TLV after receiving a MED TLV from and connected endpoint device. Not supported on the OOBM interface. The no form of this command disables support for the LLDP MED TLV. poe [priority-override] capability network-policy Specifies advertisement of power over Ethernet data link classif ication. The priority-override option overrides user-config ured port priority for Power over Ethernet. When both lldp dot3 poe and lldp med poe are enabled, the lldp dot3 poe3 setting takes precedence. Default: enabled. Syntax: lldp med [poe [priority-override] | capability | network-policy].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the lldp trap enable command do?

The lldp trap enable command enables sending SNMP traps for LLDP related events from a particular interface. LLDP trap generation is enabled by default on all the interfaces and has to be disabled for interfaces on which traps are not required to be generated. The no form of this command disables the LLDP trap generation. NOTE LLDP trap generation is disabled by default at the global level and must be enabled before any LLDP traps are sent. Syntax: lldp trap enable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Thermal Management?

Thermal Management: consists of the fand and tempd daemons, that manage the fans and temperature sensors for the system respectively. They are responsible for monitoring temperatures, adjusting fan speeds to keep the system at nominal temperatures, initiating shutdown sequences for chassis, subsystems, or transceivers during over-temperature conditions, and reporting temperatures, sensor states, fan speeds, and fan states. Subtopics Thermal management support.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Thermal Shutdown Behavior?

Thermal Shutdown Behavior: Chassis (Switch) Thermal Shutdow - Thermal shutdown is a critical sa fety mechanism designed to preve nt hardware damage caused by overheating. When critical tempe rature thresholds are exceeded, th ermal daemons log an event and i nitiate a shutdown sequence to po wer down the affected componen ts gracefully. If a temperature sensor exceeds its critical threshold and the associate d subsystem does not support bei ng powered off, the entire chassis will shut down for a five minute c ool down period. If the critical tem perature is detected again after re boot, the shutdown sequence will repeat. Platforms 8325H 9300-32C8D 9300-32D All platforms All platforms Public.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Transceiver Thermal Management?

Transceiver Thermal Management: Transceiver temperature sensors are visible on REST API calls, SNMP and CLI (show environment temperature). Events logs also are triggered upon thermal status changes, shutdown and recovery. If a transceiver exceeds the temperature threshold and it is hot swapped, the cool time timer is canceled and the transceiver is re-enabled. There is no limit to the number of times a transceiver is thermally disabled and re-enabled if it's continuously above the critical temperature threshold. This is an example of the temperature information on the normal conditions. switch# show environment temperature Temperature information ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- -- Current.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide For 8400 Switch Series

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes> command do?

The allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes> command allow non-failsafe updates of programmable devices for the specified number of minutes, or enter 0 to disallow unsafe updates. The maximum permitted value is 120 minutes. Syntax: allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state command do?

The fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state command sets the administrative state of the specified fabric module. Specifies the member and slot of the module. For example, to s pecify the module in member 1, slot 2, enter the following: 1/2 Selects the diagnostic administrative state. Network traffic d oes not pass through the module. Selects the down administrative state. Network traffic does not pass through the module. Selects the up administrative state. The module is fully operatio nal. The up state is the default administrative state. <SLOT-ID> diagnostic down up. Syntax: fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show environment fan command do?

The show environment fan command shows the status information for all fans and fan trays (if present) in the system. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. For fan trays, Status is one of the following values: • ready: The fan tray is operating normally. • fault: The fan tray is in a fault event. The status of the fan tray does not indicate the status of fans. • empty: The fan tray is not installed in the system. For fans: Public show environment fan 392 Speed: Indicates the relative speed of the fan based on the nominal speed range of the fan. Values are: Slow: The fan is running at less than 25% of its maximum speed. Syntax: show environment fan [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show environment power-consumption command do?

The show environment power-consumption command displays power consumption information. Displays detailed power consumption information. For VSF supported platforms only. Displays the power consum ption information for the specified VSF member. Range: 1-10. Power consumed values are updated every 5 seconds. The total power consumed is the total power used in a chassis. The power consumed average is calculated from the total power consumed as a running average over a period of time. The average period has a default of 10 minutes. The period can be configured using power-consumption-average-period. On chassis where power input is not supported, power consumed is displayed as Power Usage and PSU Output Power is displayed as N/A. Syntax: show environment power-consumption [detail].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show environment power-supply command do?

The show environment power-supply command shows status information about all power supplies in the switch. Display the statistic and status of the Power Supply Unit (PSU) input voltage. This parameter is supported on 5420, 6200M, 63 00M, 6400, 8320, 8325/8325H/8325P, 8100, 8360, 8400, 93 00/9300S and 10000 Switch series. Include the optional events parameter to display events for the power supply's input voltage. the output of the command displ ays information for the power supplies of the entire chassis or system (including the PSUs within a VSF member.) Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. This parameter is only available on switches with profiles that support VSX. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output from the VSX peer switch is not displayed. Syntax: show environment power-supply.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip errors command do?

The show ip errors command shows harware and software IP error statistics for packets received by the switch since the switch was last booted. If none of the optoinal VRF parameters are included, the show ip errors command displays the current system-wide hardware IP error counters and the default VRF's software IP error counters on the switch Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for a specific VRF. Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for al - VRFs IP error info about received packets is collected from each active line card on the switch and is preserved during failover events. Error counts are cleared when the switch is rebooted. Syntax: show ip errors [{vrf <vrf-name>}|all-vrfs].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Failover?

Failover: This module is a fabric module or a line module, and it is in the process of connecting to the new active management module during a management module failover event. Initializing The module is being initialized. Present The module hardware is installed in the chassis. Ready The module is available for use. Updating A firmware update is being applied to the module. Examples Showing all installed modules on an 8400 switch: switch# show module.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router-id command do?

The router-id command sets an ID for the router in an IPv4 address format. <ROUTER-ADDRESS> Required Specifies the router address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. no Unconfigures the router-id for the instance. The Router-id is changed to the dynamically selected router-id. The default ro uter-id 0.0.0.0 is updated to the routing stack that triggers to auto-elect a router-id based on the highest ip-address of l oopback interface or highest ip-address of interfaces. The co nfiguration is supported globally as well as per address-famili es IPv4-unicast, IPv6-unicast. If address-family specific rou ter-id is configured, it takes precedence over global configurat ion. Public router-id 438. Syntax: router-id <ROUTER-ADDRESS>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ospfv3-af IPv6 lsdb command do?

The show ospfv3-af IPv6 lsdb command shows the OSPFv3 AF link state database summary for different OSPF LSAs (Link State Advertisement). Use the parameters to get information for a particular LSA. Enter an OSPFv3 AF process ID to display general OSPF inform ation for a particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> Public show ospfv3-af IPv6 lsdb 482 adv-router {<ROUTER-ID>| self} Select to display link states for a particular advertising router. S pecify either a Router ID of the advertising router or specify sel f to show self-originated link states. area <AREA-ID> Select to display information filtered for the specified area in on e of the following formats. • OSPFv3 AF area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. • OSPFv3 AF area identifier in decimal format. Syntax: show ospfv3-af [<process-id>] IPv6 lsdb.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor activate command do?

The neighbor activate command this command enables the address-family capability and exchange of information specific to an address family with a BGP neighbor. The no form of this command removes the address-family capability and disables the exchange of routes for the specified address-family with the BGP neighbor. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the nei ghbor. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Public neighbor activate 604 NOTE VPNV4 address-family, peer group, and BFD are not supported for BGP unnumbered. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} activate.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor advertisement-interval command do?

The neighbor advertisement-interval command sets the advertisement interval, which defines the length of time between transmission of BGP routing updates. The no form of this command restores the default value. Default values are 30 seconds for external BGP peer and 5 seconds for internal BGP peer. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a Peer-Group. This is not supported on BGP unnum bered. Public neighbor advertisement-interval 606 Specifies the advertisement interval in seconds. Range: 0 to 60 0. Default: 30 for external BGP peer and 5 for internal BGP peer. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor ao command do?

The neighbor ao command enables TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) authentication on a TCP connection between two BGP neighbors. To disable this function, use the no form of this command. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <keychain-name> Name of the keychain for the neighbor. The keychain allows keys to be configured with different valid li fetimes. This mechanism provides a way for a set of keys to be rotated a nd hence protect against long-lived-key attacks. At any give n time only one key is selected as active-key and keys are vali d for a duration of the defined send-lifetime. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ao <keychain-name>.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what is the syntax of the neighbor ao command?
The syntax of the neighbor ao command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ao <keychain-name>

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor ebgp-multihop command do?

The neighbor ebgp-multihop command enables BGP to establish a session with external peers residing on networks that are not directly connected. By default, BGP can only establish sessions with external BGP peers that are directly connected. If external BGP peer are not connected directly, then ebgp-multihop is enabled to initiate the connection with that external peer. If ebgp-multihop is disabled and external BGP peers are indirectly connected, then BGP peer session is not established. The no form of this command disables the peer ebgp-multihop feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> <ttl>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <ttl>.

The syntax of the neighbor ebgp-multihop command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <ttl>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor fall-over command do?

The neighbor fall-over command enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Public neighbor fall-over 620 Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <PEER-GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.

The syntax of the neighbor fall-over command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor graceful-shutdown command do?

The neighbor graceful-shutdown command configures the wait time before shutting down the BGP neighbor session, and can also configure the local preference value to be advertised before graceful shutdown. Public neighbor graceful-shutdown 623 The no form of this command sets the wait time to the default value of 180 seconds and the local- preference value to the default of 0. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Specifies the local preference value for exporting the iBGP rou tes during a graceful shutdown. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown.

The syntax of the neighbor graceful-shutdown command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor interface command do?

The neighbor interface command configure a BGP neighbor by specifying the interface name on which the neighbor is connected instead of the neighbor’s IP address. When an unnumbered interface is specified to the neighbor command, a static route is auto-provisioned for the neighbor with the unnumbered interface as a destination. A static route is also auto-provisioned if the neighbor IP address is learned via LLDP. NOTE If OSPF is enabled on an unnumbered interface then the auto-provisioning is not supported as the route to the BGP peer is expected to be learned via OSPF. Peer-group is not supported for BGP peering on the IP unnumbered interface. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. Public neighbor interface 626. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} interface <ifname>.

The syntax of the neighbor interface command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} interface <ifname>

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor passive command do?

The neighbor passive command configures a specific neighbor, or all neighbors in a peer-group, as passive, which means that they will not initiate the TCP session. The no form of this command enables the neighbor to initiate the TCP session. Public neighbor passive 638 Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} passive.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor remote-as command do?

The neighbor remote-as command creates a peer, initiates the connection to the peer, and adds an entry to the BGP neighbor table. Specifies a neighbor with an autonomous system (AS) number that identifies the neighbor as internal to the local autonomous system. Otherwise, the neighbor is considered as external. By default, neighbors that are defined using this command, exchange only unicast address prefixes. Public neighbor remote-as 643 The no form of this command disables the peer session and deletes the peer information. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> remote-as <AS-NUMBER>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>.

The syntax of the neighbor remote-as command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor remove-private-AS command do?

The neighbor remove-private-AS command this command forces the BGP neighbor to drop the private AS numbers. When the outbound update contains a sequence of private AS numbers, this sequence is dropped. If the command is configured for peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will remove the private-AS before sending the BGP update message. The no form of this command allows the private-AS number to be carried in BGP update message. Public neighbor remove-private-AS 647 Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remove-private-AS.

The syntax of the neighbor remove-private-AS command is: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remove-private-AS.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor route-reflector-client command do?

The neighbor route-reflector-client command this command configures the router as a BGP route reflector and the specified peer as its client. The no form of this command disables this function. Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form at (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor shutdown command do?

The neighbor shutdown command this command disables the peer session, terminates any active session for the specified neighbor or peer group, and removes all associated routing information. This action can cause the sudden termination of many peering sessions. The no form of this command enables the peer session for the specified neighbor. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command do?

The neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command this command enables you to generate inbound updates from a neighbor and change and activate BGP policies without clearing the BGP session. Changes in BGP policies require the BGP session to be cleared which can have a large negative impact on network operations. The no form of this command disables this setting. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Public neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound 659. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration inbound.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor timers connect-retry command do?

The neighbor timers connect-retry command this command sets the connect-retry timer for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group. The no form of this command clears the connect-retry timers for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Public neighbor timers connect-retry 662 Specifies the retry timer value. <RETRYTIME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers connect-retry.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor weight command do?

The neighbor weight command this command assigns a weight to a neighbor connection. When the weight is applied to a peer-group then all the neighbors that are part of the peer-group will inherit the value configured. The no form of this command removes a weight assignment. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> <WEIGHT-VALUE> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Specifies the weigh to be associated with the routes received f rom the neighbor. Range: 0-65535. Public neighbor weight 667. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} weight <WEIGHT-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router bgp command do?

The router bgp command this command configures the BGP instance on the router, configures the AS (Autonomous System) the router belongs to, and enters into the BGP router configuration mode. Only a single BGP AS number can be assigned for the entire system. The no form of the command deletes the BGP instance from the router. Public router bgp 671 Specifies a 4-byte AS number in the range 1-4294967295 i n integer format or from 0.1-65535.65535 in dotted format. AS-NUMBER. Syntax: router bgp <AS-NUMBER>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip ospf routes command do?

The show ip ospf routes command displays OSPF routing information. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> protected Specify an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a deci mal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display neighbor information for all VRFs. Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Selects a protected route. Specifying an IPv4 address will select protected route for that prefix. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] routes.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip community-list command do?

The ip community-list command configures a community list to match a specific community number attribute. Community-list is a named list of regular expressions. They are used to match the community number attributes in the routes for inclusion in, or exclusion from route policies. The sequence number is optional and is autogenerated whenever it is not explicitly mentioned. All community-list rules with the same name are grouped. The no form of this command removes the community list configuration. <COMM-LIST-NAME> Specifies the name of the community list that matches commun ity number of a route. Specifies the description of the community list. Maximum chara cter limit is 80. Public ip community-list 723 seq <SEQ> Specifies the order of reference of the regular expression rules. Syntax: ip community-list standard <COMM-LIST-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the router vrrp {enable disable} command do?

The router vrrp {enable disable} command enables or disables VRRP protocol globally. You must globally enable the VRRP feature for VRRP virtual router. no router vrrp enable disables VRRP protocol globally but does not remove all VRRP configurations. no router vrrp disable enables VRRP protocol globally. Syntax: router vrrp {enable | disable}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply policy command do?

The apply policy command applies a classifier policy containing a PBR action to an interface. A policy with PBR actions is only applicable to L3/routing interfaces. The no form of this command removes a classifier policy containing a PBR action from an interface. config-if Specifies name of the policy. • Only Layer 3 interfaces are valid for PBR policy application, and only in the routed inbound direction. Syntax: apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the policy command do?

The policy command creates, modifies, or deletes a classifier policy. A policy contains one or more policy entries ordered and prioritized by sequence numbers. Each entry has an IPv4/IPv6/MAC class and one or more policy actions associated with it. An applied policy processes a packet sequentially against policy entries in the list until the last entry in the list has been evaluated or the packet matches an entry. If a match occurs the related entry, actions are taken. The no form of this command is used to delete a policy or an individual policy entry. Specifies the name of the policy. <POLICY-NAME> <SEQUENCE-NUMBER> Specifies a sequence number for the policy entry. Optional. Ran ge: 1 to 4294967295. comment Stores the remaining entered text as a policy entry comment. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure IRDP?

To configure IRDP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable IRDP on an interface with the command ip irdp 2. Set the maximum hold time with the command ip irdp holdtime 3. Set the maximum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp maxadvertinterv 4. Set the minimum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp minadvertinterv 5. Set the IRDP preference level with the command ip irdp preference 6. Review IRDP configuration settings with the command show ip irdp.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure IPv6 RA?

To configure IPv6 RA, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable transmission of IPv6 router advertisements with the command no IPv6 nd suppress-r 2. Optionally, configure IPv6 unicast address prefixes with the command IPv6 nd prefix 3. Optionally, configure support for DNS name resolution with the commands IPv6 nd ra dns ser.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command do?

The IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command sets a global unicast address on the interface. Public IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> 35 The no form of this command removes the global unicast address on the interface. NOTE This command automatically creates an IPv6 link-local address on the interface. However, it does not add the IPv6 address link-local command to the running configuration. If you remove the IPv6 address, the link-local address is also removed. To maintain the link-local address, you must manually execute the IPv6 address link-local command. Syntax: IPv6 address <global-unicast-address>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command controls the O-bit in RAs the router transmits on the current interface; but is ignored unless the M-bit is disabled in RAs. Configure to set the O-bit in RA messages for host to obtain network parameters through DHCPv6. The other-config-flag is disabled by default. For more information on configuring the M-bit, see IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag. Public IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag 54 The no form of this command turns off (disables) the setting for this command in RAs. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 nd router-preference command do?

The IPv6 nd router-preference command specifies the value that is set in the Default Router Preference (DRP) field of Router Advertisements (RAs) that the switch sends from an interface. An interface with a DRP value of high will be preferred by other devices on the network over interfaces with an RA value of medium or low. The no form of this command set the value to the default of medium. Sets DRP to high. Sets DRP to medium. Default. Sets DRP to low. switch(config)# interface 1/1/1 switch(config-if)# IPv6 nd router-preference high Public IPv6 nd router-preference 59. Syntax: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Interface counters polling?

Interface counters polling: • Agent IP configuration for IPv4 and IPv6 • Header size configuration • Max datagram size configuration • Ingress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Egress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Enable/Disable sFlow per interface • Support for three remote collectors • A collector can be defined on the default and non-default VRF • Sampling on Ethernet and LAG interfaces • High availability support (sampling continues to work after switch-over) • Source IP support (setting source IP for sFlow datagrams sent to a remote collector) Limitations • Sampling rate cannot be set per interface (global only) • sFlow is not configurable through SNMP. • sFlow ingress is mutually exclusive with mirroring of rx traffic and sFlow egress is mutually exclusive with mirroring of tx traffic. • When rx mirroring and sFlow ingress or tx mirroring and sFlow egress sampling are configured on the same interface, after the boot, hot-swap, or fail-over, either sFlow or mirroring feature will be active on Public sFlow agent 75 that interface depending on the timing.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear sflow statistics command do?

The clear sflow statistics command this command clears the sFlow sample statistics counter to 0 either globally or for a specific interface. Specifies all interfaces on the switch. interface <INTERFACE-NAME> Specifies the name of an interface on the switch. Syntax: clear sflow statistics {global | interface <INTERFACE-NAME>}.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the sflow command do?

The sflow command enables the sFlow agent. • In the config context, this command enables the sFlow agent globally on all interfaces. • In an config-if context, this command enables the sFlow agent on a specific interface. sFlow cannot be enabled on a member of a LAG, only on the LAG. The sFlow agent is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables the sFlow agent and deletes all sFlow configuration settings, either globally, or for a specific interface. Syntax: sflow.

source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

The syntax of the sflow command is: sflow.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the sflow disable command do?
The sflow disable command disables the sFlow agent, but retains any existing sFlow configuration settings. The settings become active if the sFlow agent is re-enabled. Syntax: sflow disable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command specifies the preferred VLAN for DHCP client options, including DNS, NTP, and Default Gateway. The preferred VLAN is the VLAN used to select DHCP client options within a specified VRF when multiple VLANs have DHCP client enabled in the same VRF. If no VRF is specified, the configuration applies to the default VRF. In cases where the preferred VLAN is not explicitly configured, the lowest VLAN with DHCP client enabled will be automatically selected. The no form of the command removes the preferred VLAN configuration and restores the default behavior. VLAN ID to set as preferred VLAN. VLAN identifier range: 1 to 4094. Specifies the VRF in which the preferred VLANshould be confi gured.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 helper-address command do?

The IPv6 helper-address command defines the address of a remote DHCPv6 server or DHCPv6 relay agent. Up to eight addresses can be defined. The DHCPv6 agent forwards DHCPv6 client requests to all defined servers. Not supported on the OOBM interface. The no form of this command removes an IP helper address. <UNICAST-IPv6-ADDR> <MULTICAST-IPv6-ADDR> all-dhcp-servers egress <PORT-NUM> Specifies the unicast helper IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the multicast helper IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies all the DHCP server IPv6 addresses for the interface. Syntax: IPv6 helper-address unicast <UNICAST-IPv6-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure a DHCPv4 server on a VRF?

To configure a DHCPv4 server on a VRF, follow the documented procedure: 1. Assign the DHCPv4 server to a VRF with the command dhcp-server vrf. This switches to the DHCPv4 2. Define an address pool for the VRF with the command pool. This switches to the DHCPv4 server pool 3. Enable the DHCP server on the VRF with the command enable 4. Configure support for persistent external storage of DHCP settings with the command dhcp-server 5. View DHCPv4 server configuration settings with the command show dhcp-server all-vrfs.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bootp <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The bootp <REMOTE-URL> command sets the BOOTP options that are returned by the DHCPv4 server for the current pool. BOOTP provides a way to distribute an IP address and boot image file to client stations. The DHCPv4 server returns the IP address and the location of the boot image file, which must be stored on an external TFTP server. The no form of this command disables support for BOOTP. <REMOTE-URL> tftp://{<IP> | <HOST>}/<FILE> Specifies the name and location of a BOOTP file on a TFTP server in the format: • <IP>: Specifies the IP address of the TFTP server hosti ng the file in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal nu mber from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For exa mple, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Syntax: bootp <REMOTE-URL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command do?

The netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command defines up to four NetBIOS WINS servers for the current DHCPv4 server pool. WINS is used by Microsoft DHCP clients to match host names with IP addresses. The no form of this command removes the specified WINS servers from the pool. Specifies the IP addresses of NetBIOS (WINS) servers in IPv4 fo rmat (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Sep arate addresses with a space. A maximum of four IP addresses can be defined. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. Syntax: netbios-name-server <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command do?

The static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command creates a static binding that associates an IP address in the current pool with a specific MAC address. This causes the DHCPv4 server to only assign the specified IP address to a client station with the specified MAC address. The no form of this command removes the specified binding. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. The IP address must be within the address range defined for the current pool. Specifies a client station MAC address (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the host name of the client station. Range: 1 to 255 pr intable ASCII characters <IPv4-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> hostname <HOST>. Syntax: static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear dhcpv6-server leases command do?

The clear dhcpv6-server leases command clears DHCPv6 server lease information. The DHCPv6 server must be disabled before clearing lease information. Clears leases for all VRFs. <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears the lease for a specific client on a specific VRF. Specify t he client address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. You can use two colons (: :) to represent consecutive zeros (but only once), remove leading zeros, and collapse a hextet of four zeros to a single 0. For example, this address 2222: 0000: 3 333: 0000: 0000: 0000: 4444: 0055 becomes 2222: 0: 3333: : 4444: 55. vrf <VRF-NAME> Clears leases for a specific VRF. Syntax: clear dhcpv6-server leases [all-vrfs | <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>] | vrf.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the pool <POOL-NAME> command do?

The pool <POOL-NAME> command creates a DHCPv6 server pool for the current VRF and switches to the config-dhcpv6-server-p ool context for it. Multiple pools, each with a distinct range, can be assigned to a VRF. A maximum of 64 pools (IPv4 and IPv6), 64 address ranges, and 8182 clients are supported on the switch across all VRFs. The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCPv6 server pool. Specifies the DHCPv6 pool name. A maximum of 64 pools (IP v 4 and IPv6) are supported across VRFs on the switch. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters. First character must be a letter or number. <POOL-NAME>. Syntax: pool <POOL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the static-bind IPv6 <IPVv6-ADDR> client-id <ID> [hostname <HOST>] command do?

The static-bind IPv6 <IPVv6-ADDR> client-id <ID> [hostname <HOST>] command creates a static binding that associates an IP address in the current pool with a client identifier or DUID. This causes the DHCPv6 server to only assign the specified IP address to a client station with the specified client identifier or DUID. The no form of this command removes the specified static binding from the pool. <IPv6-ADDR> Specifies the IP address to assign in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xx xx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal num ber from 0 to F. For example, this address 2222: 0000: 333 3: 0000: 0000: 0000: 4444: 0055 becomes 2222: 0: 3333: : 4444: 55. client-id <ID> Specifies the client identifier or DUID. hostname <HOST> Specifies the host name of the client station. Range: 1 to 255 pr intable ASCII characters. Syntax: static-bind IPv6 <IPVv6-ADDR> client-id <ID> [hostname <HOST>].

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
section: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? ?

4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? : The factory default VLAN to which the DHCP address is assigned is VLAN1. Users can configure the VLAN for DHCP allocation on platforms supporting multiple VLAN configurations. Only a single VLAN can support DHCP assignments. 5. Can DHCP assignments be disabled in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch? Use the no ip dhcp command on the required VLAN interface or management VRF port you can disable the DHCP assignments in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch. A static configuration option is available on both ports. 6. Can IP DHCP switch assignment be carried out on a Routed only Port (RoP)? No, RoP is not supported. Only VLAN and OOBM ports can be allocated an address with DHCP. 7. Can an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch support DHCP IPv6 Allocation? Yes, an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch can support DHCP IPv6 allocation. However, for Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP), IPv4 is required. 8.

topic: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping event-log client command do?

The dhcp-snooping event-log client command this command enables or disables dhcp-snooping client level event logs that help with client telemetry on a remote management station such as HPE Aruba Networking Central. By default, client level event logs are disabled. The no form of this command disables client-level event logs for DHCP snooping after they are enabled. View these logged DHCP snooping events by issuing the command show events -c dhcp-snooping. NOTE For additional information on DHCP-related event logging, please refer to the Event Log Message Reference Guide. Syntax: dhcp-snooping event-log client.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME> command do?

The dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME> command configures external storage to be used for backing up IP bindings (used by DHCP snooping) to a file. When configured, the switch stores all the IP bindings in an external storage file so that they are retained after the switch restarts. When the switch restarts, it reads the IP bindings from the configured external storage file to populate its local cache. NOTE When both external storage and flash storage are configured to store DHCP snooping IP bindings, the external storage takes priority, and is used exclusively until it becomes unconfigured, at which time flash storage (if configured) is used. Later, if external storage is configured again, flash storage stops and external storage resumes. The no form of this command disables the saving of IP bindings in an external storage file. Syntax: dhcp-snooping external-storage volume <VOL-NAME> file <FILE-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server <IPv6-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server <IPv6-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command adds an authorized (trusted) DHCPv6 server to a list of authorized servers for use by DHCPv6 snooping. This command can be issued multiple times, adding a maximum of 20 authorized servers per VRF. By default, with an empty list of authorized servers, all DHCPv6 servers are considered to be trusted for DHCPv6 snooping purposes. NOTE The mgmt VRF cannot be used with this command. NOTE Configure the link local IPv6 address instead of global IPv6 address of the DHCPv6 server as the authorized-server. For example: switch(config)# dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server fe80: : 2ca4: fa40: d4cd: bc2f The no form of this command deletes the specified DHCPv6 server from the authorized list. Specifies the IPv6 address of the trusted DHCPv6 server. <IPv6-ADDR> vrf <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping authorized-server <IPv6-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE> command do?

The preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE> command enables a DHCPv6 snooping guard policy to allow or deny the DHCPv6 servers in the specified server preference range. If not configured the minimum preference is set to 0 and maximum preference is set to 255. The no form of the command removes the server preference limits on the specified DHCPv6 snooping guard policy. minimum <VALUE> maximum <VALUE>. Syntax: preference [minimum | maximum ] <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv4 client source binding information to the switch IP binding database. Although DHCPv4 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IP binding database. NOTE Statically configured IP binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv4 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv4 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no IPv6 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv6 client source binding information to the switch IPv6 binding database. Although DHCPv6 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IPv6 binding database. NOTE Statically configured IPv6 binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. Public IPv6 source lockdown commands 324 The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv6 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv6 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns host command do?

The ip dns host command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
section: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A?

Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A: L3-GW(config)# vlan 100 L3-GW(config-vlan-100)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface 1/1/25 L3-GW(config-if)# vlan trunk allowed 100 L3-GW(config-if)# no shutdown L3-GW(config-if)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.1/24 Now the clients will be able to ping the gateway IP; but will not be able to communicate with each other due to the isolation enforced with private VLAN. As expected, the ping from client 1 to client 2 will fail in the above example, although the clients are in the same subnet.

topic: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
section: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A
source_file: 8400/10_17_1000/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A?

Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A: If local proxy ARP is enabled in the L3 gateway, the L3 gateway responds to ARP requests for addresses in the same subnet. In other words, the L3 gateway will be doing proxy for ARP resolution. L3-GW(config)# no ip icmp redirect L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip local-proxy-arp With this, the L3 communication will be enabled between client 1 and client 2. Now the ping from client 1 to client 2 will be successful in the above example. The ARP table in client 1 will be populated with the MAC of L3 gateway; not the MAC of client 2. Similar ARP table entry for client 2 as well. In other words, the L3 gateway is doing proxy for ARP resolution for the local network where clients are isolated at layer 2. Dynamic ARP inspection Dynamic arp inspection is provided as a mechanism for making ARP more secure.

topic: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, how do you configure proxy ARP?

To configure proxy ARP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config 2. Switch to an interface with the command interface, or to an interface VLAN with the command i 3. Enable local proxy ARP with the command ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear arp command do?

The clear arp command clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries from the ARP table. If you do not specify any VRF or port parameters, ARP table entries are cleared for the default VRF. Specifies a port on the switch. For example: 1/1/1. ip <A. B. C. D>|all] IPv6 <X: X: : X: X>|all vrf (Optional) Include an IP address to clear neighbor entries for th at specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries for all IP addresses. (Optional) Include an IPv6 address to clear neighbor entries for that specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries fo r all IPv6 addresses. Clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries for the specified VRF or for all VRFs. If no VRF is specified he default VRF is cleared. all-vrfs Clear neighbor entries for all VRFs Clear neighbor entries for the specified VRF. Syntax: clear arp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>] command do?

The debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>] command enables ARP security debug logs. If <SEVERITY> is omitted, all severities are logged. The no form of this command disables ARP security debug logs. Public debug arp-security 371 <LOG-CATEGORY> severity <LEVEL> Selects the ARP security debug log category. Available categori es are: • all: Selects all ARP security debug log categories. • config: Selects the ARP security config debug log category. inspection: Selects the ARP security inspection debug log • category. • packet: Selects the ARP security packet debug log categor y. Specifies how to filter the ARP security debug logging by settin g the minimum severity level for which debug logging will be pe rformed. The selected severity level and all severities above (m ore severe) will be included in the logging. Syntax: debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the destination cpu command do?

The destination cpu command the command causes the mirror session to transmit mirrored packets to the switch CPU. This destination may be configured for multiple sessions, however only one such configured session may be active at a given time. The diagnostic utility Tshark may be used to view and capture packets transmitted to the CPU through this route. Ctrl+C must be entered to terminate a Tshark capture session. More details can be found in the Supportability Guide. The no form of this command will immediately stops mirroring traffic to the CPU, but will not remove any sources from the mirror configuration. Syntax: destination cpu.

Specifies the session identifier. Range: 1 to 4 <SESSION-ID>. Syntax: mirror session <SESSION-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the VLAN-NUM command do?

The VLAN-NUM command selects the VLAN number. Specifies the direction of mirroring. tx (transmit), rx (receive), o r both. Creating a mirror session and adding a VLAN as a source of traffic in both directions on that port: switch# configure terminal switch(config)# mirror session 1 switch(config-mirror-1)# source vlan 10 both Creating a mirror session and adding two VLANs as sources of traffic: directions: switch# configure terminal switch(config)# mirror session 2 switch(config-mirror-2)# source vlan 10 tx switch(config-mirror-2)# source vlan 20 both Configuring the source in session 2 to receive by specifying the source interface configuration: switch(config-mirror-2)# source vlan 10 rx Removing the first source interface in session 2 entirely, and removing the transmit direction from the other so that mirro.

The snmpv3 context <NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> [community <STRING>] command creates an SNMP v3 context on the specified VRF. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP context. Specifies the name of the context. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASC II characters, excluding space and question mark (?). Public snmpv3 context 159 vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the VRF associated with the context. Default: default. community <STRING> Specifies the SNMP community string associated with the con text. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question mark. Default: public. Syntax: snmpv3 context <NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> [community <STRING>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID> command do?

The snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID> command configures the SNMP v3 SNMP engine-id allowing an administrator to configure a unique SNMP engine-id for the switch. This engine-id is used by the NMS management tool to identify and distinguish multiple switches on the same network. The no form of this command restores the default engine-id, created by the switch using a combination of the enterprise OID value and the switch's MAC address. SNMP v3 SNMP engine-id in colon separated hexadecimal n otation. <ENGINE-ID>. Syntax: snmpv3 engine-id <ENGINE-ID>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy} command do?

The snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy} command configures the SNMP v3 security level. The security level determines which SMNPv3 users defined by the command snmpv3 user are able to connect. The no form of this command changes the security level as follows: • no snmpv3 security-level auth: Sets the security level to auth-privacy. • no snmpv3 security-level auth-privacy: Sets the security level to no authentication or privacy, allowing any SNMP user to connect. SNMP v3 users that support authentication, or authentication a nd privacy are allowed. Only SNMP v3 users with both authentication and privacy are al lowed. This is the highest level of SNMP v3 security. Default. Syntax: snmpv3 security-level {auth | auth-privacy}.

The no form of this command sets the SNMP master agent port to the default value. Specifies the UDP port number that the SNMP master agent wil l use. Range: 1 to 65535. Default: 161. <PORT>. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the snmp-server community command do?

The snmp-server community command adds an SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. A community string is like a password that controls read/ write access to the SNMP agent. A network management program must supply this name when attempting Public snmp-server community 165 to get SNMP information from the switch. A maximum of 10 community strings are supported. Once you create your own community string, the default community string (public) is deleted. The no form of this command removes the specified SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. When no community string exists, a default community string with the value public is automatically defined. Specifies the SNMP v1/SNMP v2c community string. Range: 1 to 32 printable ASCII characters, excluding space and question ma rk. Syntax: snmp-server community <STRING>.
